data.title: Ocado prepares to scrap 1,000 jobs in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Ocado is preparing to cut as many as 1,000 jobs as part of a scramble to drive down costs.

The struggling company, which employs around 20,000 staff, is preparing to reduce headcount to save hundreds of millions of pounds.

The proposed redundancies could be approved later this month, with those in its head office most likely to be affected. However, a final decision has yet to be made.

The job cuts are being made by Ocado Group, the London-listed company that builds robot warehouses and owns 50pc of online grocery business Ocado Retail.

It comes after a turbulent year for the business, which has seen its share price fall more than 40pc in the last six months alone.

This has been blamed on the loss of crucial warehouse contracts in North America.

A source close to the business claimed the proposed job losses form part of a strategy to improve cash flow over the next two years.

The company made a loss of £375m in 2024 but swung to a profit in the first half of last year on the revaluation of a stake in Ocado Retail, the groceries business it runs jointly with M&S.

Although best known for its grocery deliveries to British households, the company also pioneered the extensive use of robots in its warehouses, a technology it offers to businesses worldwide.

However, Ocado’s shares were rocked last year after US retailer Kroger confirmed plans to close three automated warehouses that used the company’s technology.

Kroger, which has built eight sites with Ocado since 2018, said it was moving to fulfil some of its online orders from existing stores instead of using the warehouses.

Another blow came last month when Sobeys, a supermarket in Canada, announced it would shut another site using the British company’s kit.

An Ocado spokesman said: “We regularly review our operations to ensure we’re set up for long-term success. If and when decisions are made that affect our people, we are committed to communicating with them directly and ensuring they are supported throughout.”

Ocado’s potential job losses, first reported by The Times, come at a time of darkening fortunes for those seeking work in the UK, fuelled by falling vacancies and rising redundancies, which have seen unemployment rise to a four-year high of 5.1pc.

Demand for staff fell in January for the 27th consecutive month, recruiters warned, with particularly large falls in vacancies for medical, secretarial, hospitality and retail workers.

The figures from the Recruitment and Employment Confederation and KPMG raise fears that unemployment will continue to rise, despite Government pledges to boost employment and wages.

“A growing, inclusive economy requires high levels of employment – a focus on encouraging firms to create jobs rather than discouraging that investment is more important than ever,” said Neil Carberry, chief executive of the REC.

“So far, the Government has struggled to convince businesses that it wants them to hire. That has to change in the decisions that are made this year if we are to avoid a continued rise in unemployment.”

A Government spokesman said: “This Government is creating the conditions businesses need to recruit and grow by investing £1.5bn in work, training and apprenticeships while restoring economic stability and bearing down on inflation, helping interest rates to fall six times, giving firms the confidence to hire.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: BBC plots £600m of cuts with fears for jobs and programming
data.description:
The BBC plans to slash more than £500m from its annual budget in a move almost certain to involve job losses and programme cuts.

The public service broadcaster told staff it was aiming to strip out a tenth of its annual costs over the next three years. The BBC’s operating costs stood at just over £6bn last year, meaning the savings would amount to around £600m.

It is not yet clear exactly where the cost savings will be targeted but staff fear job cuts and further cutbacks to output.

Tim Davie, the outgoing director-general, said in a call with staff on Thursday that the cutbacks would affect all parts of the corporation, including its licence fee-funded public service arm and its commercial BBC Studios division.

In a note to staff, Mr Davie wrote: “To stay competitive and relevant, we need to adapt and innovate with purpose and pace.

“Over the next three years, like everybody in our sector, we will need to continue to find savings and move money to where we need it to remain relevant for our audiences.”

It is the latest in a series of demoralising rounds of cuts at the BBC, which had already pledged to strip out £700m by 2028.

The BBC has already wielded the axe in areas including radio and news, with BBC Two’s flagship evening news programme Newsnight slashed to half an hour and the HardTalk interview show scrapped altogether.

The corporation last year said it would spend £150m less on making new shows as it warned rising production costs had left it with an “unprecedented” funding challenge. In 2023, the BBC sparked outrage after cancelling daytime medical drama Doctors after 23 years.

The latest cuts were announced on the same day that a Florida judge ruled that Donald Trump’s $10bn (£7.3bn) case against the BBC will go to trial in February 2027, rejecting the broadcaster’s attempts to delay it.

A BBC spokesman said the cuts were unrelated to the case. They added: “It’s the reality of choices we have to make given ongoing funding pressures.”

The corporation is trying to plug a £500m hole in its finances, triggered by a combination of rising costs and a reduction in the number of Britons paying the licence fee.

The BBC’s licence fee income dropped by 30pc in real terms between 2010 and 2020 after a series of freezes and cuts to the levy. It has also suffered from a steady decline in licence fee payers, with a further 300,000 households ditching the fee last year.

The latest cut announcement came just a week after the Government confirmed that the cost of the licence fee will rise by £5.50 to £180 from April.

The funding crisis comes as the BBC enters into negotiations over the future of the licence fee ahead of the end of the current Charter period next year.

Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, has said she is considering a range of possible reforms, including funding the broadcaster through advertising or subscriptions.

BBC bosses have acknowledged the need for reform but have pushed back against the idea of subscriptions or advertising, arguing that the broadcaster should remain a universal service.

The BBC is expected to submit its proposals for future funding in the coming weeks with other ideas reportedly under consideration including imposing a fee for its radio stations or extending the licence fee to include shows available to stream on services such as Netflix or ITVX.

The United States president’s defamation lawsuit is a further threat to the BBC’s fragile finances. The lawsuit is centred on a misleading edit of Mr Trump’s speech during the Jan 6 2021 riots in an episode of BBC One’s Panorama, which The Telegraph revealed.

The scandal prompted the resignation of both Mr Davie and Deborah Turness, the BBC’s head of news. The BBC has named senior executive Rhodri Talfan Davies as interim director-general, beginning in April, while it searches for a permanent replacement.

A BBC spokesman said: “Over the last three years, we have delivered more than a half a billion pounds worth of savings, much of which we’ve been able to reinvest into our output across the BBC.

“In a rapidly changing media market, we continue to face substantial financial pressures. As a result we expect to make further savings over the next three years of around 10pc of our costs.

“This is about the BBC becoming more productive and prioritising our offer to audiences to ensure we’re providing the best value for money, both now and in the future.”

data.title: The City has become America’s plaything
data.description:
It is a sad yet undeniable fact that the City of London is suffering from serious decline.

It once acted as a giant beacon for trade and investment but the Square Mile has today lost much of its allure.

The financial crisis, overly burdensome regulation, a shrinking stock market, a shift in working habits and massive technological change have all contributed to the malaise.

The question that City officials grapple with is whether it is terminal or not.

Senior figures in the financial world point out that the City has faced repeated challenges during its long history – including several plagues, the Great Fire of London and the Blitz – and has managed to bounce back stronger every time.

Yet it is hard to escape the sense that this time is different, that what we are witnessing is the capital being slowly hollowed out until it represents a husk of its former self.

The news that fund giant Schroders has succumbed to a £10bn takeover at the hands of a bigger US rival must rank as one of the most crippling blows the City has suffered yet. In fact, it may be the death knell for London’s status as one of the truly global financial centres.

As if that wasn’t sufficiently humiliating, our once-great financial district is in danger of being turned into America’s plaything.

Bigger, bolder, more productive US companies – backed by a president fully behind them – are swooping on their weaker, meeker British counterparts with abandon.

The UK boasts a long list of stored financial institutions but few are as venerable as Schroders.

Indeed it is hard to think of a greater symbol of Britain’s sorry demise than the sight of the blue-blooded money manager – a business that was founded a year before the Battle of Trafalgar – surrendering more than 200 years of independence and family ownership to an opportunistic trans-Atlantic interloper without so much as a peep of protest or fight.

That the deal came on the same day the UK economy missed its growth forecasts with yet another thoroughly dismal set of results is telling.

It is not a coincidence that London’s inexorable slump comes as Britain’s place in the world becomes more irrelevant. The fate of both are inextricably linked. As London bleeds companies, corporate chiefs and capital, the UK is on course for its worst performance in a century.

Many will say the sale of Schroders is somehow a demonstration of Britain’s great strength and confidence in its prospects. This has long been the narrative that we have all been fed as our best and brightest companies have fallen into foreign hands.

Don’t be fooled by the spin: prosperous countries don’t allow their biggest assets to be sold to the highest bidder in the same way that businesses with strong, independent futures don’t flop into the arms of an admirer at the first opportunity.

Dame Elizabeth Corley, the chairman of Schroders, insists that despite quitting the stock market, London would “remain at the heart of this enlarged business”, serving as the combined group’s non-US headquarters.

Meanwhile Richard Oldfield, the company’s chief executive, deserves a medal for his attempts to talk up the sale.

“If we increase growth, London does even better than it would have done on a standalone basis,” he said, ignoring the fact that its new owner Nuveen – and Chicago, America’s third most populous city, where the company is headquartered – surely stand to benefit far more.

It is all desperately hopeful to the point of naivety. Ambitious and assertive American bosses know weakness when they see it, which is why Schroders has found itself in the crosshairs.

There is no getting away from the sense that Schroders is a fading force.

Ten months ago, Schroders shares were trading at a decade low and while the share price has staged a decent recovery, the reluctance of investors to pay a premium for market expertise when active funds are consistently being outperformed by passive competitors at a fraction of the cost has the makings of an existential crisis.

Similarly, while the Schroders family and the company’s board are having their tummies tickled, UK boardrooms are under siege from across the pond.

Arguably more concerning than the prospect of British fund management royalty vanishing is the arrival of activist fund Elliott on the shareholder register of the London Stock Exchange Group (LSEG).

This too has been greeted by something of “nothing to see here” response despite a 35pc fall in LSEG’s share price amid the threat from artificial intelligence, as well as a prolonged slump in share listings.

To read the reaction from some quarters, you would think Elliott’s approach was nothing more than a misunderstanding.

The suggestion seems to be that once it gains a better appreciation of LSEG’s evolving business model, one of Wall Street’s most feared activists will quietly sell its shares and vanish into the night. Such an assumption would most probably prove to be sorely misguided. Elliott’s campaigns usually result in some sort of shake-up even when the target resists.

No less damning of how complacent the City has become is the attack launched on multiple underperforming UK investment trusts by Boaz Weinstein, the US raider.

There are undoubtedly aspects of his assault that don’t necessarily stack up. Having adopted a scattergun approach that captures no less than seven trusts, Weinstein can have few complaints about those who paint him as a smash-and-grab merchant.

Equally, it is fair to question his chances of improving the fortunes of such a disparate bunch of firms.

However, there is more than a ring of truth to his accusation that sector performance has ranged from “underwhelming” to “disastrous”.

Even Terry Smith, the Mauritius-based fund manager who is no stranger to a dust-up, admitted that Weinstein had a point about the need for change at his Smithson trust.

Yet Weinstein’s remarks also read like an epitaph of Britain more generally as predatory American investors pounce en masse.

data.title: Netflix promises cheaper UK streaming in Hollywood takeover fight
data.description:
Netflix is to promise cheaper streaming for British viewers as it seeks to push through its $83bn (£60bn) takeover of Warner Bros.

Netflix is preparing to tell the UK competition watchdog that bundling its streaming service with the soon-to-be-launched HBO Max will result in lower overall costs for viewers.

Warner Bros has confirmed that HBO Max will launch in the UK next month at a starting price of £4.99 per month. It will carry the upcoming Harry Potter TV series alongside HBO hits such as Succession and The Sopranos.

The launch of yet another streaming service will add to costs for British households seeking to view the widest range of programmes.

However, Netflix will argue that a combined package of its own service and Warner’s HBO Max would cost less than two separate subscriptions if its deal goes through.

The emphasis on cost savings is understood to form part of Netflix’s defence as it gears up for scrutiny by the Competition and Markets Authority.

HBO Max already operates in the US, and Ted Sarandos, Netflix’s co-chief executive, told a US Senate committee earlier this month that people who subscribed to both would receive a “meaningful discount”.

It is not yet clear whether Netflix will maintain the two services as separate apps or whether HBO Max’s films and TV programmes will be available as an additional tier within Netflix.

Either way, Netflix has said it intends to maintain both brands rather than subsuming HBO Max into its own service.

Netflix’s deal to take control of Warner Bros has triggered global competition concerns. The US department of justice has also launched a review to assess whether the deal would grant Netflix monopoly power in the streaming market.

Bosses are trying to reassure regulators while also fending off a rival $108bn bid tabled by Paramount, the US media group controlled by Oracle billionaire Larry Ellison. Paramount argues that its approach offers greater certainty.

While Warner Bros has repeatedly rebuffed Paramount’s overtures, the board is reportedly now considering reopening talks with the rival bidder amid mounting challenges to Netflix’s bid.

Any such move could force Netflix to increase its offer, reigniting the bidding war.

While Netflix is framing its pitch to regulators around consumer costs, the monthly price of an ad-free Netflix subscription has risen by more than 60pc since 2019, from £7.99 to £12.99.

Mr Sarandos has refused to rule out further price rises. However, he has argued that the fact that subscribers can cancel with “one click of the button” means costs will be kept in check.

HBO Max will be the new home of TNT Sports in the UK, which has the rights to some Premier League and FA Cup matches. Subscribers will have to pay an additional £30.99 per month for TNT.

The launch means Sky will lose its exclusive access to HBO programming in the UK. Sky is seeking to counter this threat with a new package that will bundle Netflix, HBO Max, Disney+ and Hayu into a single subscription costing £24 a month.

Netflix and Warner Bros declined to comment.

data.title: AI fears doom £575m takeover of FTSE software company
data.description:
A stock market crash triggered by fears around artificial intelligence (AI) has derailed the £575m takeover of a British company.

Shares in Pinewood AI, which is listed as Pinewood Technologies, fell by 30pc on Monday after private equity firm Apax said it no longer planned to make a bid for the software provider.

Apax said it had pulled out of talks owing to “prevailing challenging market conditions”, a reference to the widespread slump in software stocks in recent weeks.

Company valuations have fallen amid fears that the rapid advancements in AI coding tools such as Anthropic’s Claude Code and OpenAI’s Codex will shake up the software market by allowing companies to easily develop their own systems instead.

Pinewood Technologies, which specialises in software for car dealerships, said last month that it was in talks with Apax over a 500p-per-share deal that would value the company at £575m.

But Apax said on Friday evening: “In light of the prevailing challenging market conditions, Apax confirms that it does not intend to make an offer for the company.”

Pinewood’s board then said on Monday it “remains very confident in the positive long-term prospects for the group” and said it would “remain at the forefront of technology innovation”.

However, the company’s share price plunged to 307p, down 30pc on Friday’s price.

Pinewood’s software allows car dealerships to manage sales, customer relationships and accounting, and is marketed as “software as a service”, with customers paying subscriptions for the tools. The company rebranded as Pinewood AI in 2024 to focus more on artificial intelligence. 

Publicly traded software businesses have endured a huge market sell-off in recent weeks. Salesforce, Snowflake and Workday have fallen between 13pc and 23pc in the last month amid fears that customers will be able to easily replicate the features of their software.

Tools such as Claude Code and Codex have surged in popularity in recent weeks, with the systems’ ability to write and test code vastly improving. The number of Claude Code users has doubled since January, according to Anthropic.

Anthropic raised $30bn (£22bn) at a $380bn valuation last week.

Birmingham-based Pinewood, a member of the FTSE 250, was created as part of a breakup of UK dealer group Pendragon. Its biggest shareholder is US car dealership group Lithia, which owns almost a third of the company.

Turbulent market conditions have hit plans for a number of planned stock market listings, including the Norwegian software group Visma, which was planning a £17bn London flotation.

data.title: Collapsed law firm investigated on suspicion of stealing clients’ cash
data.description:
Britain’s solicitors watchdog has launched a fraud investigation into a law firm after its sudden collapse put hundreds of jobs at risk.

The Solicitors Regulation Authority (SRA) is investigating potential fraud at PM Law and the possible misappropriation of client money.

PM Law unexpectedly closed this month, leaving thousands of its clients and more than 600 of its staff in limbo. The collapse led to the shuttering of 11 of its high street law firms across Britain, which PM Law had acquired over the past three decades.

The Sheffield-headquartered business owns firms in Yorkshire, Cumbria, Berkshire and Derbyshire including Butterworths Solicitors, Autonet Law and Proddow Mackay.

Jonathan Peddie, the SRA’s executive director of legal and enforcement, said: “We are investigating a potential fraud including the misappropriation of client money.

“We are focused on establishing what occurred, how it happened and who was responsible. We will take appropriate action to protect the public including enforcement action against anyone who has been involved in misconduct.”

The SRA added that it would be investigating both PM Law and individuals connected to the company. The regulator said it had already shared information with law enforcement agencies.

PM Law’s sudden collapse has caused chaos in the legal industry and left tens of thousands of clients without lawyers. The SRA said it had identified more than 100 urgent cases that had been disrupted by the closure, including active litigation matters.

Some staff reportedly turned up to their offices to find notes in the windows informing them the business had closed, while others attempted to log on remotely to find they were locked out of internal systems.

The SRA has launched a compensation fund for clients impacted by the collapse and said it was currently working to contact anyone who would be affected by the closures. The watchdog also took control of all files and client money held by PM Law earlier this month.

PM Law traces its origins back to the launch of Proddow Mackay in Maidenhead, Berkshire, in 1990. It grew into a network of law firms across the UK through a series of acquisitions of high street firms specialising in conveyancing, wills and personal injury cases.

The conglomerate was launched by a group of solicitors including Donald Mackay, who continues to own a sizeable stake in the business. Its most recent accounts from November 2025 showed PM Law had net assets of £3.1m.

Alastair James McGregor Gilfillan, one of PM Law’s original directors, was struck off in 2020 for forging a client’s signature on legal documents.

The SRA has previously been criticised for failing to spot a £60m fraud that caused the collapse of legal conglomerate Axiom Ince in 2023.

Axiom Ince had undergone rapid growth through a series of acquisitions before closing unexpectedly after a £60m hole in its accounts was discovered. At its peak, the company owned dozens of law firms across the UK and employed more than 1,400 people.

Five individuals linked to Axiom Ince were later charged with offences including fraud, forgery and the destruction of documents by the UK’s Serious Fraud Office.

Muhammad Ali, the former chief financial officer of Axiom, submitted a not guilty plea on the charges against him in February 2025. The four other defendants are yet to submit pleas. A trial is scheduled for February 2027.

data.title: Shell chief exec to become one of FTSE’s best paid bosses
data.description:
Shell’s chief executive is on course to become one of the best-paid executives on the London Stock Exchange as his company’s move away from green energy pays off.

Wael Sawan could see his total pay packet jump by £4.5m to a maximum £19m a year under proposals drawn up by the FTSE 100 company. That would make him one of the country’s highest paid blue chip executives.

Other high-earners include AstraZeneca’s Pascal Soriot, who was paid £15m in 2024, and Rolls-Royce’s Tufan Erginbilgic, whose pay is reportedly being increased to a maximum of £18m. Simon Peckham, Melrose’s chief executive, took home £58m upon retiring last year.

Mr Sawan’s base salary is just over £1.5m and the changes would mean he could earn nine times that amount per year in long-term performance pay, up from a maximum of six times currently.

That would make him eligible to receive stock awards worth £13.8m, up from £9m previously. On top of that, he can receive a maximum annual bonus of £3.8m – taking his total potential package to more than £19m.

Under Sawan, Shell has cut its exposure to green energy and focused its attention on more profitable oil and gas operations.

In November, Shell confirmed it was ditching its only two UK wind farm projects – MarramWind farm and CampionWind off the east coast of Scotland – after carrying out a business review.

Earlier in 2025, the company also revealed plans to ramp up gas sales while reducing the share of wind and solar in its power generation portfolio from 50pc to 20pc by 2030.

Shell is focusing investment in gas-fired power plants and grid-scale batteries instead, with bosses also planning to maintain oil and gas output at current levels through the end of the decade. Investors have cheered the focus on fossil fuels.

The company is already the world’s largest producer of liquefied natural gas (LNG).

Shares in the company have jumped 22pc since Mr Sawan took over in January 2023. That compares to a 0.1pc increase at BP, a 33pc increase at ExxonMobil and a 1.2pc increase at Chevron over the same period.

Mr Sawan’s proposed pay deal would still be far smaller than those of his US rivals. Darren Woods, the boss of Exxon, received $44.1m (£32.2m) last year, while Chevron boss Mike Wirth made $32.7m.

On Wednesday, a Shell spokesman said: “Every three years, Shell seeks shareholder approval for a new executive director remuneration policy as a standard part of regulations for UK-listed companies. 

“The last vote was in 2023, so this is part of the usual cycle. Final proposals will be published in the 2025 annual report on March 12 and voted on at the annual general meeting.”

data.title: Britain’s energy suppliers are still in a worryingly shaky state
data.description:
Four years on from the energy crisis that triggered the collapse of around half of UK suppliers, is our power system more secure?

You might think the answer is yes, after Ofgem introduced new rules intended to make sure energy companies are more financially resilient should future shocks occur.

But the energy regulator has confirmed that five suppliers are failing to meet its new capital requirements, up from three last year.

It has not named the suppliers. However, both Octopus Energy and Ovo Energy have both publicly confirmed they are not meeting the requirements. Both companies have recently agreed to capitalisation plans to help them meet the target.

Much smaller supplier Utilita also reported difficulties meeting the target due to seasonal demand fluctuations, but said it was short-lived and they soon went back above the target.

Under rules introduced in March 2025, suppliers are now expected to hold net assets, excluding intangibles such as goodwill, of £115 for every dual fuel customer they have, and half that sum for every customer on an individual fuel contract.

The logic of setting this spare cash aside is simple: suppliers should have enough balance sheet resilience to survive shocks in wholesale gas markets without failing and leaving consumers to pick up the tab.

Significant costs are incurred when a supplier fails, many of which are recovered through a levy on all energy bills.

In 2021-22, when half the suppliers failed, these costs ran into the billions and were loaded onto bills at a time when households were already struggling with high energy prices.

The biggest cost arises from credit balances: if a customer is in credit with a supplier that fails, the new energy company that takes on that customer must honour that balance. The “lifeboat” supplier is reimbursed for that cost through a levy on all our bills.

Another major cost is hedging – any gas or electricity purchased in advance by the failed supplier is treated as an asset in insolvency and used to repay creditors.

It cannot legally be transferred to the supplier that takes on the customers, meaning they have to go and buy contracts to cover themselves. That is another expense added to the levy.

The regime has not been without controversy. Centrica, owner of British Gas, has argued that suppliers should ring-fence customer credit balances so they are not mixed with the supplier’s own money and used to fund day-to-day operations.

Most other suppliers lobbied hard against it, and Ofgem ultimately declined to mandate ring-fencing, discouraging the use of credit balances as working capital without requiring a hard separation.

Ofgem sets out a range of restrictions it can impose on suppliers that are not on track to meet the requirement. In serious cases, it can require full ring-fencing of customer balances and restrict new customer acquisition.

The regulator does not name the suppliers with capitalisation plans nor detail anything about the plans themselves and progress towards meeting them.

The logic is that publicly naming them could create a “run on the bank” situation, where customers quit for fear of losing their power supply, (even though the regulatory regime ensures this would not happen even in the event of a failure).

This lack of transparency is increasingly hard to defend given the size of the two suppliers we know to be on the list. Octopus is the UK’s biggest supplier, with more than seven million customers, and Ovo has around four million customers.

There is no suggestion that either business is in financial trouble or at risk of going bust, but the scale of these businesses means serious thought must be given to the possibility.

Both are far bigger than Bulb, which had 1.7 million customers when it collapsed in November 2021. Bulb was considered too large for a conventional supplier of last resort process and required an expensive special administration regime to ensure customers were not left without power.

In banking, capital ratios and risk-weighted assets of major firms are published every quarter by the Bank of England to maintain confidence. Why not in the energy sector too?

Energy consumers are exposed in a similar way: their credit balances are effectively guaranteed, just as deposits are protected under the Financial Services Compensation Scheme. 

If we accept that supplier failures impose system-wide costs, then transparency should be a core part of the prudential regime, not an optional extra.

The primary role of suppliers is to handle customer money and do some accounting, which is essentially what retail banks do. 

If Ofgem’s experiment in regulation fails, it will be hard to defend its continued responsibility for the retail supply market.

data.title: Leasehold Reform Act: Everything you need to know
data.description:
Navigating the leasehold system, its confusing legalese, and bias in favour of the freeholder can be a nightmarish experience for the millions of leaseholders in the UK.

Which is why reforms that began under a Tory leadership, but will be implemented by Sir Keir Starmer’s Labour government, promising leaseholders more rights, power and protection over their homes are a welcome relief – despite the eight years it has taken for them to become law.

Running to 246 pages, the act is no easy read. However, worry not, as our guide will tell you everything you need to know about the Leasehold Reform Act and what it means for leasehold homeowners.

  • Leasehold Reform Act 2024
  • What are the implications for leaseholds?
  • When will the Leasehold Reform Act come into force?
  • When to take action as a leaseholder
  • Leasehold Reform Act FAQs

Leasehold Reform Act 2024

Making it easier, cheaper and quicker to extend a lease term, take over the management of the building or buy your freehold – these are the principles at the heart of the Leasehold Reform Act.

Here’s a summary of the measures it lays out to bring that about:

  • Standard lease extension terms will be increased to 990 years for houses and flats, from 50 and 90 years respectively.
  • Marriage value – a method of calculating the cost of a lease extension when a term falls below 80 years, which drives the bill up by thousands of pounds – will be replaced with a fairer method that is intended to make the process cheaper.
  • Leaseholders will no longer be liable to pay freeholders’ costs when they extend their lease, buy out the freehold or challenge unfair charges or poor practice at the tribunal.
  • Freeholders who manage their building themselves must belong to a redress scheme giving leaseholders a way to challenge poor practice.
  • Managing agents and freeholders must provide leaseholders with standardised service charge bills to allow leaseholders to compare and challenge costs more easily.
  • Leaseholders will no longer have to own their home for two years before being allowed to extend the term of their lease.
  • A standardised online lease extension calculator will be developed to reduce the reliance on costly valuations and create consistency.
  • More leaseholders living in blocks with commercial premises in situ will be allowed to group together to take over the management of their building – known as “Right to Manage” – as the restriction on the commercial element of a block allowed to claim this right is raised from 25pc up to 50pc.
  • A maximum time and fee will be placed on retrieving leasehold information when properties are being bought and sold.
  • The sale of new leasehold houses is banned, except in exceptional circumstances.
  • Buildings insurance commissions must be fair and transparent.

What are the implications for leaseholders?

Once the reforms are implemented, leasehold should be a fairer and more transparent system for homeowners.

One of the most significant changes, said Rob Stevens, Nationwide’s head of property risk, is the standardisation of service charge bills.

“Where leasehold gets a bad reputation is around service charges, so having that transparency is really important,” he said. “I often say to people, a high service charge doesn’t necessarily mean a bad thing, it may mean that your building is just being well maintained.

“That’s sometimes better than a low service charge, which could mean that issues are being held off and you’re getting a big bill at some time in the future. But if the bill is standardised, you’ll know exactly how your cash is being spent and weigh up if it’s value for money.”

Martin Boyd, chairman of charity Leasehold Knowledge Partnership, said changes to the rules at property tribunals has major implications for leaseholders.

“Currently, there are no circumstances in which a leaseholder has the right to recover their legal costs if they go to the tribunal.

“Landlords are always guaranteed to get some or all of their legal costs back when they’re disputing the fact they’ve done nothing wrong.

“In the future, when the secondary legislation comes forward, there’s the intention to allow leaseholders the right to make a claim for their legal costs.”

And for leaseholders with less than 80 years remaining on their lease, which can make a property difficult to sell and affect your home’s value, extending the lease should become cheaper – although the way it will be calculated has yet to be agreed on.

When will the Leasehold Reform Act come into force?

The act became law in May 2024, but the bulk of the measures have yet to be implemented.

To quote the Government, it will happen “as quickly as possible” while “taking the necessary time to ensure that reforms are watertight”.

The act itself lays out the overall framework of reforms, but that must be supported by a series of secondary legislation which provides the detail of how the reforms work in practice. While some measures have already been consulted on, such as buildings insurance fees, others have yet to be brought forward.

However, two changes have been made. Those with up to 50pc commercial premises in their building can now choose their own management company and, as of January 31, homeowners can extend their leases immediately instead of having to wait until they’ve owned the property for two years.

A group of freeholders objecting to leasehold reform were defeated in the High Court in October. They claimed that reform breached human rights and challenged the the abolishment of the current method of calculating the cost of lease extension when the term is below 80 years – known as marriage value. The Court dismissed the case in a win for the Government.

Critics have said that delays in implementation have continued after the case was settled. Harry Scoffin, of campaign group Free Leaseholders, has criticised Labour “for allowing the 2024 Leasehold and Freehold Reform Act to sit rusting”.

In January, the Government announced that ground rents – an annual fee leaseholders must pay to their freeholder – would be capped at £250. The reforms have been published in a draft of the Leasehold and Commonhold Reform Bill. This is separate Labour legislation designed to address any problems in the 2024 Act.

When to take action as a leaseholder

Given the amount of time that has passed since homeowners were promised a fairer leasehold system in the Conservative’s 2017 housing white paper, it’s difficult to say how much longer it will take before the cost of a lease extension falls.

When to take action, said Mr Boyd, depends on your own circumstances.

“If your lease has more than 80 years left to run and you don’t have ground rent giving you problems with the mortgage company, don’t do anything at the moment, wait until the legislation goes through.

“If you’ve got no option because your ground rent is too high and you can’t sell the property because no one can get a mortgage on it, then you shouldn’t wait because you’ve got no choice.”

But if your lease is under 80 years, waiting for the legislation is a gamble, said Mr Boyd, because the outcome of the judicial process and the way lease extension premiums will be calculated is not yet known.

“If you were being in any way cautious, you’d probably want to extend your lease now because you don’t know what the results are going to be in the future.”

Leasehold Reform Act FAQs

You may have the opportunity to buy the freehold of your property, but you will first need to find out if you are eligible to do so.

Lease, the leasehold advisory service, says that to buy the freehold of a house, you will need to meet certain criteria:

  • If the house has been converted into flats, you will need to have the lease for the entire building
  • When it was granted, the lease must have been for more than 21 years
  • You must be the leaseholder. Prior to January 2025 you had to have held the lease for two years – but this rule has since been abolished.

If you would like to buy the freehold of a block of flats, leaseholders can group together to buy the freehold. You would then own a “share of freehold”.

It is then a case of working out how much it will cost and instructing lawyers. The Telegraph’s guide takes you through the steps.

The majority of the measures in the act apply to Wales, however Wales will work to its own timescale in creating subordinate legislation to implement the measures.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Energy giant revives wind farm after Miliband’s £1.8bn subsidy blitz
data.description:
A Danish energy giant has revived plans for a massive North Sea wind farm after Ed Miliband promised billions more in subsidies.

Ørsted, the world’s largest wind farm operator, said it would resubmit plans for the Hornsea 4 wind farm off the east of England next year after Mr Miliband offered a much higher subsidy for the power generated.

The group blew a hole in the Energy Secretary’s net zero hopes last year by cancelling the project. It said government-backed grants under the Contracts for Difference (CfD) scheme were too low.

Mr Miliband last month announced plans to boost the annual subsidies on offer, promising them minimum average prices of £95 per megawatt hour, which is about £10 more than Ørsted would have received under a prior deal.

Another decision by Mr Miliband means the contracts guaranteeing the payments, estimated by the energy department to cost up to £1.8bn a year, were also extended from 15 years to 20 years.

Hornsea 4 is a 180-turbine project off the coasts of Norfolk and Yorkshire. It would have a capacity of 2.4 gigawatts, among the largest ever built, generating enough power for 2.6 million homes on a windy day.

Ørsted’s rethink will be welcomed by Mr Miliband. When he originally approved the project he heralded it as a key step towards decarbonising Britain’s electricity grid by 2030.

At the time, the Government had guaranteed developers £85 per megawatt hour as a minimum price for any power generated.

Wholesale power is usually well below this price so the Government makes up the difference, funding the costs via a levy on consumer and business bills.

At the time it was widely predicted across the energy industry that the price would have to rise or Mr Miliband’s 2030 target would never be met.

That belief prompted some developers to hold back schemes in the hope of getting extra subsidy – a view that has since proven to be correct.

The Office for Budget Responsibility predicts that the cost of CfD contracts for renewable generators are set to rise from £2.3bn to around £6bn by the end of the decade once the cost of new wind farms is included. All such costs will be met via a levy on power bills.

The confirmation of the restart coincided with Ørsted’s annual financial results showing a big hit on its US operations because of Donald Trump’s attack on wind farms.

The US president has claimed the machines kill whales and birds and their largely foreign ownership is a threat to national security.

Rasmus Errboe, Ørsted chief executive, said scrapping the UK wind farm last year was a sign of the company’s “disciplined approach” to spending.

“During 2025, we demonstrated this disciplined approach in relation to Hornsea 4, which we are now reconfiguring for potential future development … we continue to hold the seabed lease, grid connection, and key permits.”

data.title: Net zero backlog puts Labour’s clean energy target at risk, warns Ofgem
data.description:
Wind and solar farms seeking to connect to Britain’s electricity grid are facing “widespread delays” in a major blow to Labour and Ed Miliband’s net zero targets.

Ofgem, the energy regulator, has warned that 210 of the 340 green energy projects scheduled to connect to the grid in 2026 and 2027 are set to miss the target.

Massive amounts of wind, solar and battery capacity must be added to the system in order to hit the Energy Secretary’s target to switch Britain’s grid to 95pc clean power by 2030.

As recently as November 2024, the Government and Ofgem said they could not “stress highly enough” the importance of connecting projects slated for 2026 and 2027 on time.

But in its letter published on Friday, Ofgem said officials were “frustrated and disappointed” to discover that hundreds of wind, solar and battery projects “imperative to ensure clean power by 2030” were already late.

The regulator blamed network companies responsible for connecting the systems together, such as the National Grid, for the majority of the delays – with long waits for planning permission and supply chain issues contributing to some of the setbacks.

In a letter to the energy industry, Ofgem said it had already rejected a request from the National Energy System Operator (Neso) and the likes of National Grid to push back the connection date for some projects after they had asked for more time.

The regulator claimed that two-thirds of the delays were “either network-driven or both network- and developer-driven”.

‘Zombie’ projects

Companies hit back and blamed the slow pace of reforms to the system of connecting projects to the electricity grid.

The Government has been working for years with Ofgem and Neso to speed up connections. An outdated “first come, first serve” approach has left the queue clogged up with “zombie” projects that are not ready, delaying those that are.

Reforms were unveiled last year to reorder the queue so that projects closest to completion are given priority.

Mr Miliband’s clean power action plan, published in December 2024, said these changes were “critical” to Labour’s clean power 2030 mission and that without them “projects we need will be delayed.”

However, Ofgem said in its letter that Neso and National Grid had warned in June 2025 that there was a risk some projects would not be able to connect on time. This was then followed by a formal request to delay them in October.

In its letter, Ofgem criticised Neso and network companies for providing “insufficient information” on both the scale of the delays and their causes.

Ofgem said it was also carrying out an investigation into the delays and why they have occurred.

Neso said it shared Ofgem’s disappointment and was “working at pace” to deliver faster connection times.

A spokesman for Ofgem said: “We are working closely with Neso and the energy sector to help achieve the Government’s 2030 clean power goals.

“Last year, we approved a range of reforms to the electricity grid connections process so ready and needed projects can connect more rapidly and help unlock economic growth.

“We are disappointed that these delays have occurred and are continuing to investigate the causes and scale of the issues identified.”

On Monday, National Grid claimed the delays were “not surprising” because the Government’s grid connection reforms did not take effect immediately.

A spokesman said: “By exempting so many projects from the process in the first two years, the reforms are not truly coming into effect until 2028.

“We are now working with Neso, the Government, Ofgem and other networks to unlock the benefits of a reformed connections process for the future, delivering new connections for customers as quickly as possible, while protecting our existing and future customers from the costs of building more capacity than is needed.”

data.title: Builder denies affair cost him slice of mother’s £2.5m fortune
data.description:
A builder was all but excluded from his late mother’s fortune partly because she was “ashamed” of him for cheating on his wife, a court has heard.

Gary MacDougall, 70, had expected to split Jeanne MacDougall’s estate – valued at £2.5m – with his sister Sandra Thomas, 65, and her husband, Lloyd, after she died in April 2020.

But because of a change to her will in 2011 and a series of property sales and gifts, Mr MacDougall is likely to be left with “nil”, the High Court was told.

He is now suing at the High Court to overturn his mother’s last will, while also accusing his sister and brother-in-law of plundering her fortune while she was still alive but incapable of consent.

At a hearing this week, he was forced to deny that his mother had genuine reasons to cut him out, including her shame over him cheating on his wife, Anna, with another woman.

Alexander Learmonth KC, for the Thomases, told the court there were valid reasons why Mrs MacDougall had changed her will in favour of her daughter and son-in-law.

He said the couple had cared for her in her old age, whereas Mr MacDougall, who had worked with his mother in the family business, had a “fractious” relationship with her.

He told the court: “Like all testators, Jeanne clearly had a variety of reasons for wanting to make a new will in the terms she did.

“To some extent, it would be speculation as to what they were, and the court is not required to indulge in that, but they certainly included a sense of how well-off Gary had become, and gratitude for the time and care given her by [Lloyd] and Sandra.

“They may also have included growing irritation with Gary, whether that be his marital infidelity, sharp words in the office, infrequency of visits or otherwise. It certainly cannot be said that the 2011 will is in any way irrational.”

Cross-examining Mr MacDougall, the barrister suggested that his mother, as a member of an older, more traditional generation, was “disappointed” in her son about his affair in 2008.

“Your mother, being of the older generation and being fond of your wife, was not happy with you, was she?” he put to Mr MacDougall. “She was very disappointed with what you had done.”

Mr MacDougall replied: “She wasn’t happy, no – she was delighted when it concluded.

“She reminded me of my responsibilities, but it was a very brief fling. If she was cross with me, Mum would have let me know. She would have given me both barrels.”

Mr Learmonth claimed that Mrs MacDougall, when making her will, was also conscious of the benefits her son had already received, having ultimately become sole owner of the family business.

He had received “huge assets” and now has “a considerable portfolio of rental properties, as well as a holiday home in Cyprus and an oast house in the countryside,” he said.

Mr MacDougall insisted that his success was down to him “grafting” while his sister and brother-in-law lived “the life of luxury”, and added: “Where’s the reward?”

Family fortune built up in property

Judge Nicola Rushton KC heard the MacDougall family fortune derived from the “substantial real estate portfolio” built up ty the siblings’ father Alec, a property developer.

Harry Martin, Mr MacDougall’s barrister, said that over the years it had been made clear to the two siblings by their parents that they would ultimately receive “broadly equal financial treatment and inheritance”.

After their father’s death, the siblings’ mother made a will in 2008, which Mr Martin said amounted to a “broadly equal” split between her son and his family and her daughter and son-in-law.

But another will was then made in 2011, under which all of her four properties went to Mrs Thomas and her husband, and they received the majority of her savings.

Mr MacDougall and his sister would split the small amount that was left, but after the costs and expenses of estate administration, that is “likely to be worth nil”, the court was told.

Mr MacDougall claims the will is invalid because of “presumed undue influence” having been made at a time when his mother was elderly and dependent on his sister and brother-in-law.

‘More than £2m left bank accounts’

He is also laying claim to a share of about £1.7m of his mother’s money which he says was “misappropriated” from her bank accounts before she died, and spent by his sister and brother-in-law on themselves and their family.

Mr Martin said more than £2m left Mrs MacDougall’s bank accounts between 2012 and 2020, with less than £500,000 being attributed to spending for her own benefit.

Alleged expenditure by Mrs Thomas and her husband included “paying for their daughter’s wedding at the five-star Savoy Hotel in London, shopping on Oxford Street, flights, spending on holiday in Spain, £30,000 to the car manufacturer Jaguar and meals out at The Ivy”, the barrister said.

Mr Learmonth accepted Mrs Thomas and her husband exceeded their authority under the power of attorney, but did not do so deliberately, having misunderstood what they were entitled to do.

The case continues.

data.title: BP scraps £550m shareholder payout in scramble to cut debts
data.description:
BP has pledged to “do better” after cancelling a $750m (£549m) shareholder payout as it scrambles to cut debts.

The FTSE 100 giant, which helps fund the pensions of millions of Britons through dividends, scrapped its share buyback programme on Tuesday after battening down the hatches following turbulence at the group.

It will seek to cut billions more in costs in 2026 alongside scrapping the buyback to reduce its debt pile 

The oil giant is struggling under the weight of a $22bn debt mountain and has suffered recent turmoil following the sudden exit of its chief executive Murray Auchincloss after less than two years in post.

Albert Manifold, BP’s chairman who was instrumental in Mr Auchincloss’s exit, has hired Woodside Energy chief Meg O’Neill as his replacement.

On Tuesday, Carol Howle, BP’s interim chief executive, attempted to placate investors by saying the company would seek to improve its performance.

“We are in action and we can and will do better for our shareholders,” she said.

BP’s latest $750m buyback programme was announced in November 2025 before Tuesday’s suspension.

BP said it had sunk to a $3.4bn loss in the three months ending in December, compared to a $1.2bn profit in the third quarter of 2025, amid weaker crude oil prices.

Its underlying replacement costs profit – its preferred measure of profitability – fell from $8.9bn to $7.5bn for the year. Its replacement costs profit fell 16pc over the quarter, in line with analysts forecasts.

The latest fall in profitability included an impairment charge of $4bn on its “low carbon energy” division, part of a costly write-down of BP’s net zero bets disclosed in January.

BP had expected the oil price to be around $76 a barrel for this year but it currently trades at around $69, adding pressure to the company’s finances.

“With a continued emphasis on capital discipline and returns, we are reducing capital expenditure for 2026 to the lower end of the guidance range, while continuing to drive down our cost base,” Ms Howle said.

BP last year tore up a strategy, first introduced by former boss Bernard Looney in 2020, to focus on renewables and the green energy transition after a backlash from shareholders. This included scrapping a multi-billion pound hydrogen plant on Teesside.

The oil giant had previously intended to cut its oil output by 40pc and reach net zero emissions by 2050.

The arrival in April of Ms O’Neill, a former executive at US oil major ExxonMobil, is expected to trigger renewed focus on BP’s fossil fuels division.

BP is already increasing its oil and gas investment and exploration. It is also in the process of selling $20bn of assets.

Derren Nathan, an analyst at Hargreaves Lansdowne, said the decision to scrap BP’s share buyback was part of an attempt to “clear the decks” ahead of the arrival of Ms O’Neill as it pursues a “leaner, meaner” strategy.

Shares in BP fell 3.5pc in early trading.

data.title: Hinkley Point C’s ‘fish disco’ actually works, researchers find
data.description:
An underwater “fish disco” at Britain’s newest nuclear power station has been mocked as an example of the huge cost and bureaucracy involved with building things in the UK.

But at least the new system, designed to preserve fish species, actually works.

EDF, the developer behind Hinkley Point C plant in Somerset, on Tuesday said trials of the acoustic fish deterrent – underwater speakers that blast out sound to keep fish away, dubbed the “fish disco” – found it was “highly effective”.

Tests at sea and in laboratory tanks by scientists at Swansea University showed that only a single tagged twait shad came within 98 feet of the device when it was switched on, compared to 14 when it was off.

This suggested “an effectiveness of more than 90pc”, EDF said, although further trials will be carried out later this year.

The fish disco forms part of £700m of fish protection measures planned for the nuclear power plant, which EDF said gave the site more marine conservation capability than any comparable site in the world.

The findings by Swansea University were welcomed by activists, who said the results proved that ministers should not water down environmental standards for nuclear power.

This week, a cross-party group of some 60 backbench MPs, including more than 30 from the Labour benches, joined nature charities to warn that any relaxation of standards would trigger “unnecessary and hugely damaging environmental regression”.

However, they are being opposed by pro-growth campaigners and industry experts who have pointed to a government review that found nuclear developers faced a “fragmented” regulatory system that often imposed “disproportionate” demands with little regard to costs or trade-offs.

Hinkley’s cooling pipes will suck in around 132,000 litres of water per second from the Severn Estuary to cool the plant’s two nuclear reactors once they are operational.

According to the Environment Agency, this risks putting more than four million fish at risk of being killed. These include Atlantic salmon, herring, European sea bass and European eels, which are critically endangered.

To mitigate the plant’s impact, EDF said it had spent £700m on fish protection measures, including a special intake pipe mouth costing £500m, a fish recovery and return system for £150m and an “acoustic fish deterrent” costing another £50m.

The French state-owned energy giant said the effectiveness of these measures meant it should not have to also create a 900-acre salt-marsh nearby.

Critics of the fish protection measures at Hinkley have branded them “regulatory madness”, arguing they have added huge costs and delays to the project for disproportionately small benefits.

A review by the economist John Fingleton found the fish protection measures would save “0.083 salmon per year” along with “0.028 sea trout, six river lamprey, 18 Allis shad, and 528 twaite shad”.

Sam Richards, of pro-growth campaign group Britain Remade, said: “Blowing £700m on fish protection for Hinkley Point C, including the now-legendary ‘fish disco’, is indefensible in the extreme.

“This is just the latest piece of research that highlights the huge costs and delays Britain’s planning system and habit regulations foist on major infrastructure projects, for little gain.”

Mr Richards also pointed to findings by Swansea University that salmon identified as at risk in fact avoid the Hinkley site.

Salmon migrating to the Atlantic generally used the main channel “well away from Hinkley Point C”, researchers said. In two years, only two tagged salmon were detected within a kilometre of the intake pipes.

Mr Richards said: “After years of delay and hundreds of millions spent, we’re told fish barely go near the intakes at Hinkley anyway. This isn’t environmentalism, it’s regulatory madness.”

However, Matt Browne, of the Wildlife Trusts, defended the measures and accused opponents of misrepresenting the number of fish saved.

Mr Browne said: “We are happy with the acoustic fish deterrent, combined with the other two measures.

“What we would like to see is for EDF to stop belly-aching. This is a hugely important stretch of water for fish, including protected species, and yet there have been persistent suggestions that somehow this is all unnecessary.”

Some 50,000 endangered eels alone were at risk of being killed each year without the measures, Mr Browne said.

He added: “We say this is good news – it is effective mitigation and an example of the rules working as they should do.”

Ministers are expected to set out plans for simplifying nuclear regulations in the coming weeks.

data.title: The battle over ‘mad’ plans to redevelop Liverpool Street station
data.description:
On a cold night this week as the rain lashed down, campaigners gathered in an unassuming east London office block to thwart a £1.5bn revamp of Britain’s busiest station.

The Liverpool Street Station Campaign (Lissca) – a “supergroup” of heritage enthusiasts spearheaded by TV presenter Griff Rhys Jones – is fighting plans to demolish and rebuild key parts of the railway terminus and linked Underground station.

The group this week unveiled its alternative vision, one they claim will help to modernise Liverpool Street station without destroying its history.

The rival plan drawn up by John McAslan + Partners, the architects behind the transformation of King’s Cross station in 2012, will be presented to local officials in the coming weeks.

“If you’re coming from Italy on a train to Liverpool Street station, would you rather see a brand new gateway ... or something which says Victorian, Britain and great railway heritage?” Rhys Jones says.

“If you break it, it’s gone, you can’t get it back. It could be a much richer experience, without doing an awful lot.”

Network Rail say expansion is needed to cope with the expected increase in passenger numbers at a station already at capacity. They argue only a wholesale overhaul will do.

Campaigners, meanwhile, claim that plans to modernise the station would ruin a Victorian landmark and create years of needless disruption.

The fight over Liverpool Street station, which opened fully in 1875, is a microcosm of the tug of war between heritage and modernisation that threatens to hamper Labour’s ambitions for a construction boom.

Since taking power, Sir Keir Starmer has vowed to make it more difficult for people to oppose major building projects.

He has pledged to override the “whims of Nimbys” and back the builders rather than the “blockers”. But in cases like Liverpool Street, these statements have only galvanised the opposition.

Al Watson, head of planning at Taylor Wessing, says: “The situation is more tense now than it was four years ago because the previous government leaned more towards heritage.

“This Government, by its very nature, pre- and post-election, is pro-development. That’s the vision, that’s the instruction, and that’s why it is more tense now than it has been in a long time.”

Until now, Lissca has faced down each proposal to redevelop Liverpool Street station. The group in fact dates back to the 1970s, when British Rail’s initial bid to demolish the terminus was thwarted by Lissca, then fronted by the poet Sir John Betjeman.

More recently, plans drawn up in 2023 by architects Herzog and de Meuron were shot down after the group helped mobilise 2,200 objections and 26,000 petition signatures.

“We’ve won battle after battle,” says Sir Simon Jenkins, founder of the Railway Heritage Trust and a supporter of the group.

The new plans, ordered by Network Rail and designed by architects Acme, will involve partially demolishing the station to expand its concourse.

A 19-storey, 97m glass office tower would be built above a neighbouring listed former hotel, while gothic yellow brick vaults would arch over the station’s entrances.

Over 150 million passengers by 2041

Network Rail argues that expansion is essential.

The opening of the Elizabeth Line, which passes through Liverpool Street, has helped the station become the UK’s busiest in each of the past three years.

There were 98 million rail entries and exits last year, and Network Rail predicts this will grow to 158 million by 2041.

Planning officials for the City of London Corporation are currently reviewing the revamp proposals, with councillors expected to vote on them on Feb 10.

The scheme has so far attracted more than 2,275 letters of objection and 1,135 in favour.

The February deadline has galvanised Lissca into action, with the group hoping their alternative plans will convince the City of London to reject Network Rail’s plans.

“What we have to do over the next 20 days … is do what we can to make sure that [the committee is] aware that the City of London deserves better than this current scheme that is before them, and they have the right to ask for it to be better,” Jones says.

‘Illogical’ plans

While Network Rail’s designs will evoke the station’s Victorian heritage, Lissca believe they can retain far more of the original building.

They estimates its plans will take half the time and cost half as much as Network Rail’s proposals, with almost no demolition required.

“The reason it’s so high is because of the void costs, created by the demolition of so much fabric,” says McAslan. “It’s so illogical, it’s mad.”

Lissca’s vision includes a green walkway inspired by the Eden Project, with Sir Tim Smit, founder of the Eden Project, advising McAslan on landscape strategy.

A vaulted office building would sit at the northern end of the station’s platforms, with nine floors of timber hanging from a lightweight steel frame to provide around 700,000 sq ft of workspace.

McAslan believes Network Rail’s plans will cause 12 years of disruption for travellers – a timeframe he calls “completely unacceptable”.

But Network Rail says this timeframe is a gross exaggeration.

Work on modernising the station itself will begin as early as 2028 and be completed in about five years, with development of offices above the passenger concourse taking three more.

While ticket barriers will be moved and shops demolished to enlarge the concourse, the impact on people using the station will be limited to the “mild inconvenience” of having to use different entrances at certain times, Network Rail says.

Officials argue the project should be no more disruptive than the modernisation of Paddington at the turn of the century and far removed from the upheaval that accompanied the upgrade of London Bridge station.

The five-year, £1bn project to transform London Bridge, the capital’s oldest rail terminus, resulted in delays and dangerous levels of overcrowding.

A Network Rail spokesman said: “It’s very much not going to take 12 years and it won’t be on the scale of London Bridge because none of the work being done is to create new tracks and platforms.”

Network Rail commissioned an independent team to review McAslan’s preliminary scheme but concluded it was undeliverable owing to complexity, funding challenges and the potential for prolonged station closures.

Its review concluded that building offices over the train shed – as proposed in the McAslan plan – would be too costly and structurally challenging because Network Rail does not own land to either side.

It would also require “significant closure” of the station to build a deck over live lines.

Back in the war room at McAslan’s Farringdon offices, campaigners are undeterred.

“There’s a critical mass of buildings which create a sense of the Victorian past in that part of the city, and it must be preserved,” says Sir Simon.

“Just remember, there’s always a choice. Nothing is inevitable. Fight on.”

data.title: Higher water bills are a shameless reward for failure
data.description:
On the face of it, the news that water suppliers won’t be allowed to ramp up bills by as much as they were hoping is cause for celebration.

At first glance, it looks like an all-too-rare rebuff for an industry that has been thumbing its nose at customers for far too long – which is, of course, precisely how this latest sorry charade is meant to be interpreted.

Ministers are desperate for us all to think that after decades of wrongdoing, it has taken a Labour government to finally get tough with the cowboys that pollute our rivers and charge us a fortune for the privilege, all while absentee shareholders and negligent executives line their own pockets with money that could’ve been spent on investment.

Yet, that isn’t what is happening here. Not even close.

Yes, bills won’t be increasing to the extent that suppliers were asking for – but the point is that an independent panel of “experts” has still sanctioned further rises beyond what Ofwat had approved and most objective observers thought those rises were already pretty steep.

So the idea that this is somehow a big victory for households and a defeat for water companies is profound nonsense. The opposite is true.

In fact, it is impossible to see further bill hikes as anything other than a reward for failure for an industry that has long treated customers and the environment with complete contempt.

Not that Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, seems even remotely prepared to acknowledge what is really happening here.

“We are laser-focused on helping ease the cost-of-living pressure on households. We’ve frozen fuel duty, raised the minimum wage and pensions and brought down mortgage rates, putting more money in people’s pockets,” she said – though hopefully not with a straight face.

This is gaslighting territory. It takes a special kind of shamelessness to boast about making people better off at the precise moment that millions of families are having a fresh round of bill rises imposed on them.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said on Thursday it is allowing five water companies – Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, Southern Water, South East Water and Wessex Water – to lift bills by up to 5pc over the next five years.

That might seem fairly tame if it wasn’t for the fact that it comes on top of an average increase of 24pc that industry regulator Ofwat had already approved in April.

As Mike Keil, chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), pointed out: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.”

In fact, two in five of the households these companies serve say they will struggle to afford the increases coming down their pipe, according to the CCW, which represents water customers across England and Wales.

What’s more, the CCW has conducted its own analysis showing there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs to cut bills by around £41 a year – “but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return,” Keil said.

However, you would’ve thought otherwise when reading Hardy’s hollow words on Thursday.

“I understand the public anger over bill rises – that’s why I expect every water company to offer proper support to anyone struggling to pay,” she said.

But as Keil said: “Three of these five companies also have no plans to end water poverty by 2030.”

The CMA doesn’t come out of this much better. It makes a big song and dance about how the additional funding will ensure “essential improvements”, such as more resilient supply and a reduction in pollution, are delivered “at reasonable cost”.

Most people will accept some level of inflation in their household expenses but I’m willing to bet that few regard a 27pc surge as reasonable – even if it is over five years.

Some customers are already grappling with rises close to, or more than, 50pc once inflation is added because suppliers have been outrageously front-loading increases to help pay for long-overdue infrastructure improvements.

The reality is that bill-payers are being asked to subsidise an era of playing catch-up because water suppliers failed in the past to keep pace with investment, maintenance and environmental duties.

The money was there but instead of being spent modernising the system, repairing antiquated pipes and ensuring sewage wasn’t pouring into our waterways, it was being lavished on greedy investors and weak-kneed bosses or vanishing straight out the door in the form of heavy debt repayments.

Research from the University of Greenwich has found that 35p in every pound spent on customer bills is swallowed up in debt and dividend costs.

With the system buckling, it is surely no coincidence that these are the highest bill increases since privatisation.

It is also no surprise that the suppliers that went crying to the CMA are also among the most debt-laden in a sector that has long been leveraged up to the gills.

Meanwhile, the competition policeman quietly concedes that alongside a much-trumpeted round of additional investment, bill increases will also help pay down debt and boost returns for investors – many of whom are the same faceless funds that presided over the current wretched state of affairs in the first place.

For all the talk of long overdue reform and a new era of genuine accountability from Labour, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that our broken water industry has effectively been handed a huge taxpayer-funded bailout.

data.title: Florence flats so ugly they’ve triggered a police investigation
data.description:
Not far from the banks of the Arno River, an ultra-modern block of upmarket flats nicknamed the Black Cube casts a gloomy shadow over the butter and cream-coloured villas lining the embankment.

“Brutto [ugly],” says resident Stefano Pieri, shaking his head as he peered across the river at the dark, burnished brass facade of the building in the heart of Florence. “Some might say it is beautiful modern architecture when it stands alone. The problem is it is completely out of context here.”

The building is indeed out of place – so much so it is now subject to a police investigation, dragging in everyone from architects to councillors. Even the city’s nobility have intervened, breaking cover to stop any further developments from spoiling a skyline that has changed little in hundreds of years.

The Black Cube – officially called the Teatro Luxury Apartments – is a stone’s throw from the US embassy and the Santa Maria Novella train station, and includes smart restaurants, spas and underground parking. The modern palazzo, managed by the Star Hotels chain, offers 150 short-term rental units for high-income students and professionals.

Florentine prosecutors are now questioning 12 people who are under investigation for irregularities in the permitting, planning and zoning process that paved the way for the Black Cube to rise high above the former site of the Teatro Comunale. They are trying to trace how the property was decommissioned and sold in 2013 by the then Matteo Renzi city administration.

What they know so far is that the property passed through a state entity then was auctioned off to an Italian investment subsidiary. From there it was sold to a joint venture formed by property giants Blue Noble and Hines, within the Future Living fund managed by London-based Savills. Hines maintains it constructed the building with all the necessary permits in place.

Here in the centre of the Renaissance city of the 14th-century Palazzo Vecchio, Giotto’s Campanile and Brunelleschi’s Dome, residents cannot even change the shade of their window shutters without an extensive bureaucratic permit process.

People are now questioning just how a US property developer was granted permission to build something locals also refer to as The Monster of Corso Italia.

A powerful group of ancient noble families has called for a moratorium on further high-end developments down river from the Black Cube in a former industrial area near the former Leopolda railway station.

In a letter to the mayor, the 16 noble families said: “We must save Florence from any other ‘black cubes’. No more violence toward our city.” Some of the letter’s signatory families have owned property in the city since the Medici era (1434 to 1737).

Convincing them to speak out has been called Operation Blue Blood by local media, and is largely thanks to a lobbying effort by Roberto Budini Gattai, an architect nicknamed the Communist Nobleman, who has been at the forefront of the protests.

According to Italian media, the letter of protest was drafted over an elegant lunch, where the members of the ancient families finally agreed to sign up to the cause. Among the signatories is Claude Marie Agnès Cathérine d’Orléans, 82, a French princess and former Duchess of Aosta, who married Prince Amedeo of Savoy in 1964 and has lived in Florence ever since.

The Princess is not one to mince her words. “The Black Cube and the Social Hub on Viale Belfiore are monstrosities,” she told the Corriere Fiorentino. “I apologise to the architects who designed them, but that’s the way it is. I don’t understand why, when you do something new, you don’t try – I don’t mean copying – but perhaps taking inspiration from older buildings.”

It is the question on everyone’s lips here. “Its obviously not for Florentines,” said Jacopo Palorni, 37, as he walked along the riverbank with his wife and toddler in a stroller.

Palorni and Maria Christina Silvestre are among many residents who have been forced to move out of the historic centre of Florence and find somewhere more affordable on the outskirts. Like many, they feel their concerns about the urban future of their city go mostly unheeded. They said: “When they began construction we sent several emails, but never got any response.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Vauxhall owner to restart sales of diesel cars in Britain
data.description:
The owner of Vauxhall has resumed the sale of diesel cars in Britain as the company reels from a £19bn writedown from its shift into electric vehicles (EVs).

Stellantis confirmed it is bringing back previously discontinued diesel models in Europe and will sell existing ones for longer as part of a drive to focus on profits.

The move comes after the car giant – which also owns Peugeot, Fiat, Citroën and Jeep – cancelled the launch of new EVs and announced plans to bring back hybrids. Executives described it as a shift back towards the “real-world needs” of drivers.

Since the end of 2025, the car giant has quietly reintroduced diesel variants of vehicles such as the Peugeot 308, Opel Astra, Citroen Berlingo and the DS 4 across Europe, according to Reuters.

In the UK, this does not seem to have happened yet for most of the group’s brands.

However, a diesel version of the Peugeot 308 does appear to have gone back on sale in Britain since December, internet archives show.

The company’s website says the cars will be available again from “March 2026”.

Other vehicles, such as the Vauxhall Astra – the British version of the Opel Astra – are not listed as being available as a diesel in the UK yet, despite its European equivalent going back on sale.

Diesel cars accounted for around 5pc of new car sales in 2025, although this figure also included mild hybrids, according to the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders (SMMT). That is down from 20pc in 2020.

Asked about the change in strategy, a spokesman for Stellantis told Reuters: “We have decided to keep diesel engines in our product portfolio and – in some cases – to increase our powertrain offer.

“At Stellantis, we want to generate growth, that’s why we are focused on customer demand.”

It emerged just days after Stellantis announced a €22bn (£19bn) writedown as its boss claimed the company had lost sight of “real world” drivers in its shift to EVs.

Antonio Filosa, Stellantis’s chief executive, blamed the one-off charge on “poor execution” and “the cost of overestimating the pace of the energy transition that distanced us from many car buyers’ real-world needs, means and desires”.

He also said the launches of some electric models would be cancelled and vowed to instead revive hybrid versions of best-selling cars such as the Fiat 500.

In the past year, carmakers including Volkswagen, Ford and General Motors have written $55bn (£40bn) off their balance sheets amid disappointing EV sales and government policy changes in the US and Europe.

Stellantis was formed from the merger of Fiat Chrysler Automobiles and the PSA Group in 2021, creating one of the world’s largest car companies with a large stable of well-known brands.

Since then, its market share in Europe has shrunk from 20pc to 14pc as the company has battled production delays, quality issues and growing competition from China.
