data.title: Ed Miliband plots crackdown on wind farms ‘gaming’ power system
data.description:
Wind farm owners who earn huge payouts from gaming Britain’s energy system face a crackdown under new reforms proposed by power grid bosses.

The National Energy System Operator (Neso) warned on Wednesday that companies were deliberately clogging up Britain’s energy system to boost their own revenues.

This is because wind farms can be paid to switch off when the grid becomes too congested to transport their power, allowing traders to game the system.

Neso, which handles the day-to-day operations of the electricity grid, said it was concerned about the sale of power generated by Scottish wind farms to buyers in the South, even when traders know that grid congestion means the power physically cannot be delivered.

There is currently no penalty for doing this, but it means Neso must constantly intervene to balance the system.

For example, it will pay Scottish wind farms to switch off while also paying another power station in the South to generate replacement electricity.

Under the current model, the wind farm and the power station could also be owned by the same company, allowing that company to distort supply to earn payments.

Neso warned the system had created an incentive for companies “to aggravate the constraint” as this “maximises the market participant’s expected revenue”.

It now wants to force generators to sell power from individual power stations, making it more obvious when the system is being gamed.

It also wants to require generators to settle their plans further in advance to minimise last-minute changes to the system.

Balancing costs, which can easily amount to millions of pounds per day, are ultimately passed on to households through their bills.

The problem threatens to push up household bills in a blow to Ed Miliband’s promise to cut them by £300 a year.

Mr Miliband, the Energy Secretary, last year scrapped reforms that supporters said would have ended such market gaming practices following an intense lobbying campaign by wind farm owners, who argued it would torpedo investment.

The amount spent on balancing the grid hit £2.7bn last year alone and is expected to reach between £5bn and £8bn a year by 2030 without action to tackle the problem, according to Neso.

Neso warned: “The misalignment between the national wholesale price and locational balancing needs, along with the scale and predictability of congestion, presents significant risks of strategic positioning against constraints.

“This risk is more likely to arise if the network becomes more congested.”

RenewableUK, which represents wind farm owners, claimed the problem affected “all types of power generation”.

A Neso report last year said most constraint costs were focused in Scotland, where Britain’s wind farms are most concentrated.

A RenewableUK spokesman added: “The industry is working closely with the Government to ensure that innovative solutions are rolled out at speed, such as new energy storage capacity, to add more flexibility into the system”.

A Department for Energy Security and Net Zero spokesman said: “After years of historic underinvestment, we are delivering the biggest upgrade in Great Britain’s electricity network in decades to minimise constraint costs, delivering clean power by 2030 and helping bring down bills for good.

“We are working closely with Neso, Ofgem and industry to further improve the system as part of our Reformed National Pricing package.”

data.title: The French town that puts Britain’s new homes to shame
data.description:
Before she moved to Clamart, Elena Harty had never had much pride in anywhere she had lived.

London had felt too busy and dangerous; Paris too impersonal, its old apartments too poky and temperamental.

But the new town of Clamart Panorama, a former brownfield site just 8km southwest of the French capital, ticks all the boxes.

It has schools, parks, restaurants, a wine bar and a boulangerie all within a 10-minute walk. There are clubs and societies for all age groups; lush green spaces for joggers and dog walkers. It has an abundance of flowers, a network of cycle routes and a direct tram line into Paris.

“We’ve even got a heron,” says Harty, who is 33 and moved from Britain after meeting her partner.

The couple were initially drawn to the area in 2021 by its proximity to the capital and its spacious mid-rise apartments, which imitate the ornate 19th-century mansion blocks that line Paris’s grand boulevards.

In the years since, they have watched Clamart grow into a thriving town of some 5,000 residents. “There are people from really all walks of life,” says Harty.

Affordable housing accounts for 30pc of apartments, but they are mixed in the same blocks as private dwellings, which has given rise to a diverse and close-knit community.

It stands in stark contrast to recent projects in Britain, which have faced criticism for creating drab, low-quality and characterless “ghost towns” full of houses but lacking community and social spaces.

Clamart florist Marie Paolini says her clientele are mainly young professionals and families who have moved from Paris in search of more space. “It’s a really dynamic place,” she says. “We are right next to the tram too, so we get a lot of customers.”

During the day, locals stop and chat between trips to the café and cheese shop, while at night the tables at Bistrot Papa Pool and pizzeria La Bella Vita buzz with hungry diners.

Adrien Lorente, who runs Clamart wine bar La Cave Lelay, says it has started to become difficult to get a table without booking ahead. “We are always full,” he says. “I work from 3pm until midnight and we’re full every time.”

Construction of Clamart Panorama began in 2017 as part of plans to relieve pressure on Paris’s housing market by creating new commutable suburbs for workers.

Work is still underway, with completion of its third and final phase expected for early 2027. But it has already started to become a blueprint for new towns across France.

Britons unconvinced on new-builds

It is a far cry from the latest new-build efforts in Britain. Often derided for being soulless, ugly and lacking a civic heart, the UK’s recent developments have struggled to win public approval.

A 2023 survey by the Chartered Institute of Building found that 40pc of Britons think new-build housing lacks character, while a third considers it poor quality.

New towns built in the last 10 years such as Ebbsfleet, in Kent, and Sherford, in Devon, have faced considerable backlash after homes flew up without any of the most basic amenities such as shops and GP surgeries.

The 3,000-home new town Cranbrook, in Devon, earned the nickname “Crimebrook” after an alleged rise in anti-social behaviour among bored teenagers. But other new developments have simply been accused of being eerily quiet or “dystopian”.

It is a concerning trend as Labour pushes forward with plans to build 12 new towns across Britain, as part of its pledge to build 1.5 million homes by 2029.

Ike Ijeh, of think tank Policy Exchange, says problems in Britain lie in its dogmatic approach to density.

Whereas mixed-used apartment blocks of between four and eight storeys are a common feature of European towns and cities, British developers have tended to prioritise low-rise suburban developments. Nearly 80pc of homes across England are a house or a bungalow, according to the latest census data.

“We idealise culturally in this country the idea of a house – far more than the idea of a flat,” he says. “Developers are aware of the old adage: an Englishman’s home is his castle. That really differentiates us from the Continent.”

But low-rise sprawl carries problems for both developers and the people who live there.

Financially, it is an inefficient use of space. For a similar amount of land required for a two-storey house, a developer could build a mid-rise block containing dozens of apartments and sell them off at more affordable prices.

It also encourages a homes-first, amenities-later approach that often leaves the construction of retail and hospitality spaces delayed until certain population thresholds are met. This means early arrivals can find themselves effectively living in a “ghost town” where they are surrounded by nothing but other houses.

In Clamart, on the other hand, shops, restaurants and local services occupy street-level spaces in many of the residential buildings. This is something Guy Courtois, head of urbanist think tank Pour une Renaissance Urbaine, says is crucial to its success.

“Single houses wouldn’t work. You need density,” he says. “If you want to have a lively town you need to have shops at a floor level, beautiful architecture, parks and greenery. We have all those things here.”

Where mid-rise blocks dominate, easy access to nature is fundamental. In Clamart, balconied apartments are turned towards an expansive central lake, which helps provide a constant sense of the outdoors.

Ijeh says that because mid-rise buildings achieve higher density using less space, developers tend to be able to build in more green space.

“Mid-rise is so efficient at providing density it means there’s more land available for landscaping, compared to what you would have in a low-rise and to a degree in high-rise scenario where you need lots of areas around tall buildings,” he says.

The tram, which takes just 20 minutes to travel into Paris, also plays a crucial role in making the area attractive to investors.

“Bus routes can be changed or cancelled,” says Eleanor Broad, at Create Streets think tank. “Whereas a tram line gives developers confidence about investing in the area.”

‘The key for success is a good vision from a mayor’

Clamart Panorama was not the first development in France to adopt new standards of urban planning.

Just a few kilometres down the road is Le-Plessis Robinson, a much larger town of 30,000, which laid the foundations for Clamart back in the late 1980s.

Swathes of the town’s post-war concrete tower blocks were pulled down and replaced with buildings in the neo-traditional style after new mayor Philippe Pemezec pledged to reverse the area’s decline.

Putting beauty and community at the centre of the project, Pemezec’s vision was a success. Its population rebounded, retail vacancy rates improved and visitors from around the world flocked to see the town’s transformation.

However, like Clamart, it hinged on a factor distinct to France: the authority of city mayors.

In Britain, the majority of urban planning decisions are made by elected planning committees. In France, the responsibility falls to one of the country’s more than 34,000 local mayors, who wield significant influence and control over developments in their commune.

“The number one factor for success is a good vision from a mayor, because they have the power to block any project,” says Courtois.

This means city halls can acquire land at relatively low prices, helping to keep down the overall cost of projects and support the execution of more ambitious ideas.

“Otherwise the mayor can just say, ‘If you don’t want to sell it to me, you’ll do nothing with the land – because I will block anything else’,” says Courtois.

When the city hall bought the land to build Clamart Panorama, it was a brownfield site owned by energy giant EDF. The city hall paid €50m and auctioned off lots to developers for a total of €100m. This gave them €50m to reinvest back into local services.

Watering down the original masterplan is also a lot more difficult than in Britain, where arguing about the cost or speed of construction can often persuade local authorities to loosen design codes. In France, a mayor with a strong vision can simply tell the developer that unless they follow the masterplan to the letter, they will have to sell their lot to a company that can see it through.

Courtois estimates that less than 1pc of new-build projects in France adhere to the neo-traditional principles of Clamart or Le Plessis-Robinson, despite costing just 10 to 15pc more to construct than modern developments.

But the developments are not short of detractors. Neo-traditional architectural styles continue to be dogged by accusations of being pastiche and of creating “toy towns”, and Clamart is not immune to such criticism.

Although the town has a similar look to the 19th-century buildings in Paris, some say the modern methods of using concrete frames and reconstituted stone facades appear less sturdy than Baron Haussmann’s load-bearing limestone structures. The materials also tend to weather differently to real stone, which can make buildings look shabbier over time, rather than adding character.

However, Courtois believes such criticism is largely immaterial, particularly when compared to the towns that post-war modernism provided. “Ask the people who live here,” he says. “If they are happy, then it’s a success.”

data.title: Accountant fined for using AI to cheat on exam about AI
data.description:
A senior accountant has been fined after using artificial intelligence to cheat while completing a training course about AI.

A partner at KPMG Australia was handed a A$10,000 (£5,200) fine after putting documents into an AI tool to find answers.

It was one of 28 cases of AI cheating that the accountancy firm said it had detected in the last year after introducing new techniques for checking if staff are relying on tools such as ChatGPT.

KPMG said the unnamed partner was carrying out AI training last July, which included a manual containing information on how to complete the course.

It said the person had uploaded the document into an AI tool, in violation of company policies. The attempt had been discovered by the firm’s own AI detection tools.

An internal investigation concluded that the partner should be fined more than A$10,000, which would be taken out of their future pay.

The matter is also under investigation by Chartered Accountants ANZ, the professional accounting body.

Andrew Yates, KPMG Australia’s chief executive, said: “It’s a very hard thing to get on top of given how quickly society has embraced it.”

Speaking to the Australian Financial Review, he said: “As soon as we introduced monitoring for AI in internal testing in 2024, we found instances of people using AI outside our policy.

“We followed with a significant firm-wide education campaign and have continued to introduce new technologies to block access to AI during testing.”

Mr Yates added: “Our tests are open book knowledge checks following internal training courses. People are allowed to download training materials to assist with the test. 

“But they are prohibited from uploading those materials into AI tools to assist them do the test.”

AI is causing ruptures across accountancy and consultancy firms, with growing concerns about the technology being used to cheat during exams and the potential for it to significantly cut the cost of company audits.

KPMG International, the umbrella organisation that oversees the company’s local partnerships, recently argued that its own auditor, Grant Thornton, should give it a significant discount since AI can now be used to automate a substantial amount of audit work.

Last October, Deloitte’s Australian division said it would refund the Australian government after charging A$440,000 for a report that turned out to be generated by AI and contained several errors.

KPMG Australia said it would include details of how often its staff had been caught using AI to cheat on exams in its annual report. The other 27 staff who were caught were below manager level.

Multiple industries have been hit by major stock market sell-offs in recent weeks as a result of the perceived growing threat from AI tools.

They have included enterprise software companies, financial managers and price comparison websites.

In Britain, the Financial Reporting Council has put auditors under pressure to make sure they are not allowing AI to be used to cheat during exams, with firms warning staff that they could face dismissal over incidents.

data.title: Starmer is putting his political survival ahead of the national interest
data.description:
Back in 2015, during the so-called “golden era” of Sino-British relations, Xi Jinping was welcomed to Britain for a full state visit.

Between the pomp and ceremony, he was taken to the University of Manchester facility where graphene – an extremely strong and thin material – had been invented.

The Government wanted to showcase to Xi what we were capable of and persuade him to open China’s huge markets to our products. By all accounts, Xi left impressed.Partnerships with China’s Engine Aero Corporation and Central South University soon followed.

The problem, however, was that he left far too impressed. Years later, it emerged that Beijing had sent a student to study at our National Graphene Institute. After finishing his PhD, he transferred to Beijing’s National University of Defence Technology and worked on key projects for the Chinese military. Soon after, China’s latest attack helicopters were fitted with graphene armour.

The naivety ended up backfiring economically too. Although graphene was invented in Britain, fewer than 2 per cent of graphene-related patents are registered here today. Around 60 per cent are registered in China.

The idea that engagement would influence China in a more positive direction was no less misguided. On his visit Xi was gifted a box set of Sherlock, then a firm British favourite on Chinese TV, and sunk pints at the Plough pub near Chequers.

But the fake bonhomie proved utterly pointless. The Golden Era was followed by oppression in Hong Kong, mass incarceration in Xinjiang and massive increases in military activity around Taiwan.

Starmer knows the dark reality of our relationship with China better than anybody. He will be regularly briefed by our security agencies on their industrial-scale espionage. He will have read the reports on their currency manipulation, dumping and intellectual property theft. And he has seen the way China weaponises economic integration to bully our allies who refuse to submit to them. When Australia rightly called for an investigation into the origins of Covid-19, Beijing slapped them with crippling tariffs.

So, why, given all this, has Starmer gone cap in hand to Beijing? The short answer is he is desperate.

His blunt calculation is that he needs their money. And he needs it now. The Chancellor’s coffers are empty. Growth has ground to a halt. He’s after some private investment that might inch up his meagre growth figures by a few decimal points.

Fighting for his personal survival, he cares not one jot about the long-term consequences.

Undermined our Armed Forces

Of course, improving his own lot at the expense of his country’s long-term security is nothing new for this Prime Minister. His legal career was founded upon so-called human rights claims that undermined our Armed Forces. He claims he was obliged to accept such work but as The Telegraph revealed this week, he worked with the disgraced solicitor Phil Shiner for free.

His work “challeng[ing] the government over the reach of its anti-terror legislation and taken on the British military”, according to The Guardian, led to his appointment as Director of Public Prosecutions and subsequent political career.

The question is, will Starmer be allowed to repeat the trick of enhancing his own career at the expense of his country’s interests?

To boost our country’s growth and living standards, the Prime Minister doesn’t even need to fly to Beijing to offer himself as tribute.

One of the fallacies of globalisation was that manufacturing could be outsourced to the East, but Britain could remain a powerhouse of innovation. The idea was that companies like Dyson could make their products overseas, but bright sparks here could continue to design them all thousands of miles from the production line. It doesn’t work like that. The two are too inextricably linked, as every Western economy has now learnt to its cost.

Starmer needs what Nigel Farage and Reform are championing: reindustrialisation. Since 2003 our heavy industry has halved. It’s left our economy weak, lopsided and vulnerable. It’s this industrial malaise that underlies much of our decline.

The maladies behind it are well rehearsed in these pages: the highest energy prices in the developed world, sclerotic planning, and insane net zero targets to name but a few. Ending these drag anchors on our economy and returning to making and building things would require the Prime Minister to stand up to those he fears far more than a dictator; Ed Miliband and his backbenchers.

The path back to growth lies not in Manchurian capitulation but Mancunian innovation. We still have some of the most innovative and entrepreneurial people in the world. We just need a Government that will see them, not our adversaries, as the key to our future.

data.title: Daily Mail owner’s Telegraph takeover faces four-month investigation
data.description:
The proposed takeover of The Telegraph by Lord Rothermere, the Daily Mail owner, will face a four-month initial investigation by regulators over fears it would hand him too much sway over the press.

Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, confirmed that the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) and Ofcom would have until June 10 to deliver their preliminary assessments of the £500m deal.

The 16-week schedule is double the statutory requirement of 40 working days, albeit there is a pre-notification phase in addition to that. The referral comes more than three weeks after Ms Nandy said she was minded to involve regulators.

The CMA and Ofcom will examine whether the combination of The Telegraph with Lord Rothermere’s existing news portfolio threatens the public interests in a plurality of ownership and a plurality of views. As well as the Mail titles online and in print, he owns the freesheet Metro and the i.

Lord Rothermere has said he plans to invest in The Telegraph’s international growth and backed its editorial leadership. He has argued that the explosion of news sources online means there should be no plurality concerns over a merger.

But in letters to DMGT, Lord Rothermere’s holding company, and to the independent directors currently overseeing the Telegraph, Ms Nandy’s officials said that the digital reach of the proposed combination was grounds for concern. DMGT titles were accessed by 53pc of UK internet users and The Telegraph by 37pc in July last year. They also warned that print remained influential.

The letters said: “This provides grounds to be concerned that the merger has the potential to result in the loss of a significant and distinct voice in the online news market and a concentration of control.”

The officials appeared to clarify an earlier stance from Ms Nandy that there was an “acute” risk of loss of plurality “especially in the UK’s Right-leaning market”, which it is understood to have sparked protests from DMGT that it potentially prejudiced investigations. The latest letter from Ms Nandy’s officials said any political definition of the markets under scrutiny was a matter for regulators.

If regulators identify a threat to the public interest in their initial investigation it is possible that Lord Rothermere could offer concessions, such as legally binding undertakings on the editorial independence of The Telegraph from the Mail titles, or the sale of Metro and the i.

That could avoid a full “phase two” investigation by the CMA, which could result in the takeover being blocked and heavy costs for DMGT and potentially RedBird IMI, the seller. That could take an additional six months and, with administrative interludes, extend the uncertainty into 2027.

RedBird IMI is a collaboration between the United Arab Emirates and the US private equity firm RedBird Capital. Its own attempt to gain control of The Telegraph was blocked nearly two years ago following a cross-party outcry and legislation banning foreign state ownership.

It has since made repeated faltering attempts to recoup its £500m investment in an onward sale at a price which few investment bankers, media executives or analysts can justify.

However, Lord Rothermere has made two previous attempts to buy The Telegraph and DMGT believes it is uniquely positioned to extract cost savings from back office and commercial functions that will underpin its investment.

The initial “phase one” investigation of Lord Rothermere’s bid may not end until after the three-year anniversary of the beginning of The Telegraph’s ownership saga. In early June 2023, Lloyds Banking Group seized control of the company from the Barclay family over their unpaid debt of £1.2bn.

RedBird IMI and the UAE derailed a planned auction by paying off the debt in a complex transaction intended to deliver them control of The Telegraph. Instead, Redbird IMI has been barred from exercising influence despite having its money tied up in the company.

The leisurely regulatory schedule appears to signal a change of posture from the Government. In November Ms Nandy said her “particular concerns are that the protracted uncertainty has been detrimental to the stability of the Telegraph and its staff” and spoke of the “need for the period of uncertainty to be ended”.

Lord Rothermere’s proposed takeover is the fifth attempt to secure new ownership and now faces significant additional uncertainty.

The political and market backdrop now includes attempted Labour leadership coups from the Left and a renewed focus on press regulation, as well as a steep downward swing in the valuations of companies viewed as exposed to AI.

The takeover is to be financed by a £400m loan from NatWest and a £100m deferred payment to RedBird IMI, due within two years. Credit ratings agencies have warned that DMGT faces a downgrade as a result of the transaction and the greater exposure to the digital challenges faced by news publishers, which could increase its cost of borrowing.

Despite intervening in the proposed takeover, Ms Nandy has not yet given RedBird IMI permission to transfer ownership of the option it holds over The Telegraph to DMGT, meaning no money has yet changed hands.

Ms Nandy’s officials said she was “keenly aware of the financial difficulties facing the newspaper industry”.

A spokesman for DMGT said:“We welcome the Secretary of State’s decision to issue a public interest intervention notice, which marks a significant step forward in our proposed acquisition of The Telegraph.

“As the SoS has recognised, the news media industry is changing rapidly, and publishers must compete with an array of online sources. Against this backdrop, trusted news organisations will play an ever more critical role, and that requires publishers with the resources and experience to compete.  

“We remain committed to investing in The Telegraph and its journalists, preserving its distinctive editorial voice and team, and accelerating its global expansion, with a focus on the US. 

“We look forward to working constructively with regulators and the government to secure a timely completion of the transaction, which will provide stability and certainty to The Telegraph after a protracted period of uncertainty.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The banks are booming — one guess what Reeves will do next
data.description:
Profits at NatWest and Lloyds are up. Barclays is doing well enough to reward its chief executive with a £15m pay package. Spain’s Santander is making more money than ever from its British operations.

It is great to see at least one sector of the British economy doing well. There is just one problem. As the Government shifts Left, it will be impossible to resist another raid on the sector – even though it will damage the whole economy.

The economy may have stagnated and business confidence may have fallen to record lows, but at least the high street banks are doing better than ever.

NatWest confirmed the revival on Friday when it reported annual profits up by 25pc at £7.7bn, capping an earnings season that has seen all the major British lenders report excellent results.

It is not hard to understand why all the major banks are reporting such strong profits. Interest rates are positive, households are so worried about their finances that savings are surging, and the property market is resilient.

Small businesses may be struggling to cope with a blizzard of tax rises imposed on them by a Government that doesn’t understand how hard it can be to keep a company afloat, but we have not yet seen a full-scale recession or the kind of mass closures that trigger huge losses for the lenders.

Against that backdrop, the banks should be making money. And they are.

The problem is that this is also going to make them an obvious target for the Government.

Over the past week, with Sir Keir Starmer clinging desperately to power, it has become painfully clear that Labour is going to shift significantly to the Left. It has nowhere else to go.

The backbenchers will demand it, and the main challengers to the Prime Minister, from Angela Rayner to Andy Burnham, are all positioned to Sir Keir’s left, while the moderate right of the party has been fatally weakened by its foolish association with Peter Mandelson.

At the same time, Rachel Reeves is still desperate for money, both to keep borrowing under control, and to offer the extra welfare spending and pay rises for the public sector that are the only things that Labour MPs and party activists seem to believe matter.

With the economy stagnating and contracting when measured by GDP per capita, the tax rises she has already imposed are going to raise less revenue than the Treasury forecast.

It is not hard to work out what is going to happen next. By the summer, yet another “black hole” will have opened up in the Government’s finances.

With the manifesto pledge not to raise any of the three main taxes still in place, a stealth tax on business will be one of the few ways to raise money.

The conclusion is that a windfall tax on the banks looks grimly inevitable. Indeed, in the run-up to the last Budget, the trade unions and a string of Left-wing think tanks were already arguing for a raid on the financial sector.

This year, the demands will be even louder. And yet that will turn into one more self-inflicted disaster.

Sure, the banks may appear to be making huge profits.

But that doesn’t mean this is the right time to hit them with taxes on top of the 25pc corporation tax they already pay, the 3pc “surcharge” on profits above £100m, along with all the National Insurance contributions they pay for their staff, the rates owed on their branches, and of course all the taxes paid by the businesses they lend to.

In reality, the banking industry still faces plenty of serious challenges. The app-based banks, such as Monzo, are still growing quickly, and their expansion is a direct challenge to the traditional giants on the high street. Artificial intelligence will only accelerate that.

It will take a lot of investment in technology for the old-school banks to hang on to the customers they already have – never mind find new ones.

Their costs are still far too high, and political opposition to closing expensive branches is intense, and it will only get worse as soaring taxes mean almost every kind of business – from shops to cafés – closes down.

The tech giants such as Apple, Amazon and Meta have not managed to crack the finance market yet, but that does not mean they won’t keep trying, and with payment systems on every smartphone they will keep chipping away at the margin.

Unlike retailing or media, banking has proved surprisingly resilient to digital disruption, but it may only be a matter of time.

Perhaps worst of all, the UK may soon face a major recession if the global economy or the stock market crashes, and with a spectacularly incompetent Government doing everything possible to destroy confidence in Britain’s prospects.

The banks will need plenty of investment to deal with all those challenges and to keep some capital in reserve to ride through a recession.

If the Government decides to confiscate all the spare cash they make in the good years, then it won’t be available when times are tougher.

The bumper profits the high street banks are making this year won’t necessarily last.

In reality, Britain needs to get away from the idea that success always has to be punished with extra taxes, or that we can get richer by confiscating wealth instead of creating it. A healthy economy needs a healthy banking industry.

The Chancellor will come under intense pressure over the next few months to impose a windfall levy on the UK’s finance industry.

The headline numbers may even offer some justification for that, even if it hardly stands up to a moment of serious scrutiny.

However, it would be a serious mistake – and the entire British economy will suffer if it happens.

data.title: Thames Water returns to profit thanks to soaring household bills
data.description:
Higher household bills have helped Thames Water return to profit, even as the struggling utility giant saw its debt pile grow to £17.6bn.

Britain’s biggest water supplier posted profits of £414m in the six months to September, compared to losses of £149m over the same period last year.

This swing stemmed from revenues rising by 42pc to hit £1.9bn in the first half of the year, prompted by the regulator’s decision to approve significant increases in utility bills.

However, despite the improved financial performance, Thames Water admitted it had nearly maxed out a £1.5bn loan facility from creditors, while also highlighting a 5pc increase in debt.

It also warned of an increase in customer complaints owing to higher fees, after Ofwat, the water regulator, allowed Thames Water to increase average bills from £436 to £588.

‘Rise in customer complaints’

Chris Weston, the chief executive, said the utility business’s financial performance had “improved with strong revenue growth, driven by the regulated price rise”.

He said the improvement in its finances had allowed it to spend more on fixing leaks, pollution and water quality, with capital investment up by 22pc to nearly £1.3bn.

He said: “This investment has been funded by higher customer bills, which in turn have led to a rise in customer complaints.”

It comes as the company battles to avoid nationalisation, struggling under the weight of its £17.6bn debt pile.

A group of the company’s most senior creditors are currently pursuing a potential rescue deal, which is deemed one of the last available routes to stop Thames from entering a taxpayer-backed special administration regime.

Thames said on Wednesday that its reliance on debt had increased over the six-month period.

However, it said it had also increased investment in clearing blockages, relining sewer networks, repairing leaks and installing acoustic sensors.

Mr Weston said: “This progress has all been achieved as we also manage the recapitalisation of the business.

“We continue to work closely with stakeholders to secure a market-led solution that we believe is in the best interests of our customers and the environment.

“This, in turn, will allow the transformation of Thames to continue, a programme that will take at least a decade to complete and will restore the infrastructure and operations of the company.”

He added: “The first half of this year has been shaped by good progress across all areas of our operational transformation.

“We saw a 20pc drop in pollution, and leakage performance is holding steady despite the extremely dry summer.”

data.title: Chinese EVs will destroy jobs unless Government acts, supplier warns
data.description:
European nations risk losing millions of jobs unless they stem the flood of cheap electric cars from China, the new owner of UK car parts supplier Dowlais has warned.

David Dauch, the chief executive of US-based Dauch Corp, said that an influx of electric vehicles (EVs) from the Far East threatens to wipe out vast swathes of suppliers in the West.

He said: “Countries are going to have to put the right policies in place to make sure it’s a level playing field for the suppliers and manufacturers in each of those countries.

“If not, then they run the risk of losing jobs and potentially losing industries.”

Europe’s automotive industry employs about 14m people across carmakers and suppliers.

Mr Dauch said Europe will have to consider whether to follow Donald Trump in effectively blocking Chinese EVs from the US through a combination of 100pc tariffs and strict regulations.

He said: “Right now, they are banned from coming into the US because of national security and other reasons.

“The US has put certain things in place, tariffs or other measures, to prevent them from coming in because we’re trying to protect the manufacturing base and jobs in our country.

“Other countries have to assess that on an individual basis and make a decision on what’s best for their citizens and the GDPs in each of their economies.”

Almost 28pc of the EVs sold in Britain last year were Chinese following an aggressive push by brands including BYD, Jaecoo and Omoda.

Dauch Corp’s £1.16bn purchase of GKN Automotive’s holding company, Dowlais, will create a global leader in components that transmit power to a car’s wheels such as driveshafts, differentials and axles.

The US firm, which changed its name from American Axle last month, caters mostly to the internal combustion engine vehicles that remain dominant in North America.

GKN – which will be known by its original name following the abolition of the Dowlais brand – is also a leader in EV drive systems.

Mr Dauch said the enlarged firm will adopt an “agnostic” stance on EVs versus traditional vehicles, claiming that the rush to move on from petrol and diesel engines had been overdone.

He said: “Several countries have tried to move too fast when they weren’t properly set up to support that.

“I think the UK was one of those and the US was one of those. Things were politically motivated. There are certain ways that people are trying to drive policy.

“Many countries have already gone back to review their initial policies, several have already made a decision to push those out, others have not. I think it’s going to be dependent on what the impact is on the manufacturing base for each country.”

Mr Dauch said that the Trump administration’s elimination of EV incentives had caused its market share fall from a peak of 10pc to around 5pc.

He said that makes it extremely unlikely that battery, plug-in hybrid and fuel-cell vehicles will capture 50pc of US sales by 2030, as targeted by Joe Biden, the former US president.

“I’m not saying it won’t happen over time but the time is going to be much longer than people think,” he said.

Mr Dauch said that while EVs are doing better in Europe, expecting the average driver to switch to models that cost more and have a limited range and insufficient charging infrastructure was to deny market forces.

He said: “The consumer is the boss and the only way they are going to buy a vehicle is if it’s affordable, safe and they can charge it.”

The Dowlais name was adopted in 2023 when GKN Automotive was spun off from GKN Aerospace by owner Melrose.

data.title: London’s listed landlords have been unloved for too long
data.description:
Questor, The Telegraph’s investing column, takes a weekly view of the markets – what is moving them, what lies ahead and how all of this could affect your portfolios and financial goals. 

In the wake of the global financial crisis, the big banks traded at a discount for what seemed like forever. The market had given up on them and the narrative was working strongly against them.

This, of course, did not last forever.

During 2021 and 2024, the gold mining industry became a prime target for takeovers thanks to bargain-basement prices, and many deals were struck at a hefty premium.

This, too, did not last forever.

Today, one of the London Stock Exchange’s least loved sectors has a narrative working strongly against it: hefty discounts, lowly valuations and plenty of takeover interest.

So great is the interest that an £8bn bid by the Swiss Zurich Insurance for Lloyd’s of London syndicate manager Beazley is being drowned out by a paltry £150m deal.

Even though the Zurich bid equates to a 63pc premium to Beazley’s undisturbed, pre-bid share price, this column – and more than a few UK equity fund managers and analysts besides, based on recent conversations – are paying more attention to the smaller deal.

This is the cash-and-stock bid from British Land for Life Science Reit. At around £150m, representing a proposed 21pc takeover premium, the putative transaction is surely not as eye-catching.

But this is just the latest in a string of bids for UK real estate companies, and it may well be time to give the nation’s stock market-listed landlords a little more love.

Property plunge

Over the last five years, only nine of the 38 industrial sectors that comprise the FTSE 350 index have fallen in value. Real estate investment trusts (Reits) and real estate investment and services are two of them, with respective falls of 19pc and 21pc over that span.

(The best performer is aerospace and defence, with a 539pc capital gain, before dividends, followed by banks with a 232pc advance.)

It is easy to explain the wretched showing of Reits.

From an operational point of view, bricks-and-mortar retailers face an ongoing onslaught from online rivals to the detriment of demand for high street sites, while the rise of hybrid working is a threat to demand for offices. Meanwhile, the overall economic outlook remains murky.

The steady increase in interest rates between 2021 and 2023 may have crimped demand, hurt net asset value (Nav) calculations and made Reits’ dividend yields look less attractive relative to cash or Gilts.

These issues are all well known.

Less well known is that the property trade press is starting to report shortages of prime London office space. Interest rates still seem to be on a downward trajectory and Reits as a group trade at a sizeable discount to their latest reported Navs.

The current average discount across the sector is some 23pc, but the range is wide, from around zero, at Primary Health Properties and Sirius Real Estate, to more than 40pc at Unite Group, Workspace and Town Centre Securities.

The range partly reflects different target markets between the Reits – which is also why they require careful research – but the gap between price and value is attracting predators.

Sometimes these are trade buyers, as was the case when Belgium’s Shurgard bought Lok’n’Store in 2024, others are financial buyers, such as when investment group Blackstone snapped up Warehouse Reit in 2025.

There have been 13 bids made for London-listed real estate stocks in the past three years. The average bid premium is 24pc, with the highest north of 40pc. The average discount to historic Nav per share represented by those offers is just under 12pc, a figure much closer to asset value than the current sector valuation.

Mind the gap

Experienced investors know that buying a stock in the hope of a bid is a mug’s game. The real point is to buy well-run, well-financed businesses that have a strong competitive position at a sensible price, and tuck them away for the long term so dividends can be farmed (and if possible reinvested). Any takeover is simply a bonus.

But when the takeover activity is reminiscent of that seen in the gold mining industry during 2021 and 2024, and the lowly valuations also sound like an echo of the big banks, it would be foolish not to pay attention.

Eventually canny management teams started to buy back bank stock.

We are seeing the takeovers but not, intriguingly, the buybacks in the Reit sector. Perhaps Saba’s arrival on the share register of both Life Science Reit and Workspace will prompt further action, of one kind or another.

data.title: The auto industry’s gamble on electric cars has turned into a catastrophe
data.description:
We are already getting close to the €100bn (£87bn) mark and the total is still rising.

Stellantis – the company that controls Fiat, Peugeot and Chrysler – announced on Friday that it was writing down €22bn on its electric vehicle unit. The bill is only the latest in a series of enormous losses suffered by the major American and European auto manufacturers who jumped on the EV bandwagon.

In reality, it turns out that electric cars are only a small part of the overall market and that, insofar as it exists, Chinese manufacturers will capture most of the sales.

The gamble on electric cars has turned into a catastrophe and it will be many years before the industry recovers.

With less than four years remaining until the original target date for banning the sale of all new petrol and diesel cars, the giants of the industry were meant to be riding a boom in sales of battery-powered vehicles by now.

Sleek new models would be rolling off the production lines, new battery plants would be creating hundreds of thousands of jobs, while the billions poured into investment would be the catalyst for reindustrialising both Europe and the United States.

“We’re going to need 70,000 skilled people just to make batteries across this country,” announced Boris Johnson, the former prime minister, back in 2021. He promised unlimited government support for British EV production.

Over in France, Emmanuel Macron, the French president, was pouring billions into making his country a force in battery and EV production.

So we are starting to see the results of all that investment, right? Sales are booming, profits are rising and new jobs are being created? Well, not exactly.

Stellantis’s €22bn write-down on Friday, combined with plans to offload a stake in a Canadian battery joint venture, sent shares down 27pc on the day. They have now more than halved over the last year, taking Stellantis’s value down to just €17bn.

Unfortunately, it is far from alone. General Motors said last month that it was taking a $7bn (£5bn) charge against earnings, almost all of which was accounted for by its EV unit.

Just before Christmas, Ford said it was taking a $19.5bn hit on its EV business, including cancelled vehicles and a battery joint venture. Volkswagen has started scaling back production of EVs amid heavy losses.

Porsche, which had said that electric vehicles would account for 80pc of profits by the end of this decade, instead announced a €3.1bn charge late last year after scaling back those targets.

Even Tesla, a company that knows more about electric cars than any of its rivals, is starting to struggle. It has scrapped the iconic Model S and Elon Musk, its chief executive, is desperately trying to convince everyone that robots are the future instead.

Antonio Filosa, the chief executive of Stellantis, conceded that the company had overestimated “the pace of the energy transition that distanced us from many car buyers’ real-world needs, means and desires”.

It is a painful admission but one that is at least honest. One point is surely clear. We are not hearing very much about how the transition to EVs would lead to an industrial renaissance any more.

There have been two major problems. First, EVs may only be a niche product.

Drivers are worried about the range, it is far from clear they are better for the environment once the impact of all the raw materials in the manufacturing process is taken into account, the charging infrastructure is not in place and we don’t generate the electricity to power them all at a price cheap enough to make EVs cost-effective.

Next, where there is a market, the new breed of Chinese brands led by BYD is walking away with it.

The traditional auto giants thought the transition was just a matter of replacing an internal combustion engine with a big battery, but it turned out that an EV was a piece of electronics with wheels attached. It has much more in common with the mobile phone market than anything the petrolheads running the industry were familiar with.

It is far easier to create a new EV company from scratch than to convert one of the traditional giants.

The result? All the auto companies are now in deep trouble.

Even worse, under the direction of Ed Miliband, the fanatical Energy Secretary, Britain is pressing on blindly with the 2030 target for phasing out sales of new petrol cars even as the rest of the world recognises that it is complete madness.

Indeed, only this week, the boss of South Korea’s Hyundai warned that sticking to the target threatened “severe disruption” in the UK auto market.

The blunt truth is this: the massive bet that the auto giants took on EVs has backfired spectacularly.

It has cost them tens of billions in capital, it has wasted five years of time and energy that could have been more profitably deployed elsewhere and – perhaps worst of all – it has allowed Chinese manufacturers that no one had ever heard of to establish new brands. It will very difficult to get rid of them now.

It is one of the worst self-inflicted calamities of recent industrial history.

To start the recovery process, the manufacturers will have to persuade governments to stop imposing ridiculous top-down targets that only distort the market. They will have to ruthlessly get rid of units that don’t make any money. They will have to wean themselves off subsidies and, most crucially, they will need to reconnect with their customers, making cars that people can afford and want to drive again.

After the madness of the last five years, it is the only way back – and it will be a long, hard and expensive road.

data.title: Traffic lights should turn green for cyclists, says Lime bike boss
data.description:
Traffic lights should automatically turn green for cyclists, according to the boss of Lime bikes.

Hal Stevenson, policy director for Lime, claimed it could reduce the number of cyclists ignoring red lights.

Transport for London (TfL) last month unveiled plans to give buses priority at all 3,500 traffic lights they pass in London by 2030.

Mr Stevenson called for the measure to be extended to bikes, saying: “Cyclists shouldn’t be left on red.”

Critics described the suggestion as “farcical”.

Mr Stevenson stated that TfL had extended London’s cycling network from about 90 km (56 miles) in 2016 to 430 km (267 miles) last year, with traffic light changes “the natural next step”.

“Rethinking how traffic lights are timed could be a natural next step in building on that success,” he wrote in an article for MyLondon. “Other leading cycling cities like Copenhagen and Amsterdam show what’s possible.

“Green-wave technology, which rewards cyclists travelling at a safe, consistent speed with a sequence of green lights, has improved safety and rider behaviour while reducing journey times at relatively low cost. It could also help address red-light running.”

He said traffic lights were currently timed “around motor traffic speeds”, which “led to frequent stopping for people on bikes”.

“Cycling now accounts for up to one in five trips in central London, with cycle traffic at peak times exceeding cars,” he added.

“Despite this, signal timings remain set around motor traffic speeds, which can lead to frequent stopping and less smooth journeys for people on bikes.”

Steve McNamara, general secretary of the Licensed Taxi Drivers Association, said the policy would be “farcical” because London is “incredibly congested” with traffic “slower than it’s ever been”.

He claimed people who hire bikes are “incentivised” to take “dangerous risks” such as ignoring red lights because the companies’ time-based pricing means it is cheaper if journeys are completed more quickly.

Mr McNamara said he attended road safety meetings alongside members of the “white, middle-class cycling lobby” whose “whole obsession is removing vehicle traffic from the roads”.

He added: “Has it not occurred to them that some people either can’t or don’t want to cycle?

“It doesn’t mean there’s anything wrong with them.”

A recent report by location technology company TomTom found London was the world’s slowest capital city to drive in last year.

It took an average of three minutes and 38 seconds to drive one kilometre (0.6 miles) in the centre of London last year, according to the analysis.

This was partly blamed on widespread 20mph speed limits.

Reducing speed limits from 30mph to 20mph has been one of Sir Sadiq Khan’s key transport policies as part of his ambition to reduce road casualties.

A spokesman for the mayor said journey times on the TfL road network “remain at similar levels to those in 2019” and “studies have shown 20mph limits and zones may improve traffic flow and cut traffic jams”.

A TfL spokesman said: “We already take cyclists, as well as pedestrians and other traffic, into account when we review the timings of around 1,200 signals each year.

“We’re also working to transform the technology behind London’s traffic signals, moving towards an intelligent adaptive traffic signal system that optimises the amount of green time for everyone.”

data.title: Murdoch to take over printing of The Guardian
data.description:
Rupert Murdoch will take over printing The Guardian after the newspaper’s current printer closes down most of its production sites.

The newspaper is currently printed by Reach, which owns titles including the Mirror and Express. However, Reach this week outlined plans to shutter two of its three remaining print sites in an effort to cut costs.

The overhaul means that the majority of Guardian newspapers will now be printed by Newsprinters, a joint venture between Mr Murdoch’s News UK and Daily Mail publisher DMGT, at its site in Broxbourne, Hertfordshire.

A smaller number of copies that are currently printed at the Reach site in Glasgow will be transferred to Scottish publisher DC Thomson.

The transfer, which is due to be completed by July under a 10-year outsourcing agreement, puts The Guardian’s print operations under the control of one of its most deep-seated ideological opponents.

The enmity between the two publishers dates back to The Guardian’s key role in uncovering phone hacking at Mr Murdoch’s News of the World.

The Guardian’s reporting, led by Nick Davies, exposed the widespread interception of voicemails and police bribery by journalists at the tabloid.

The fallout led to the closure of the News of the World in 2011 as well as the high-profile Leveson inquiry into press ethics.

Mr Murdoch’s empire has paid out more than £1bn to settle claims and legal expenses relating to phone hacking.

The saga was dramatised last year in The Hack, an ITV series starring David Tennant and Toby Jones.

The Guardian has also attacked Mr Murdoch and his grip on the British media in its own pages. News UK owns The Sun and The Times as well as broadcasters including Virgin Radio and Talk.

Opinion columns in the Left-leaning newspaper have previously accused the nonagenarian media mogul of “brainwashing” Britain and leaving a “toxic legacy” that gave rise to Donald Trump and the alt-Right.

The closure of Reach’s print sites comes as the news industry adapts to a sharp decline in newspaper circulation as audiences shift online. This has been compounded by the surging cost of newsprint and other inflationary pressures.

Piers North, the chief executive of Reach, said in a message to staff earlier this week: “Given the current customer demand for printed products across the UK and trajectory for the future, I believe now is the right time to consolidate our printing work, to continue to serve our customers and deliver on our strategy.”

In 2024, News UK and DMGT were granted approval by competition regulators to merge their printing operations into a single company.

At the time of the deal, the publishers said the combined company would print on average over three million newspapers each night and almost 22 million per week. The Telegraph is also printed under contract by Newsprinters.

A source close to The Guardian stressed that its contract remained with Reach despite the deal to outsource to Newsprinters.

The Guardian pursued a costly print overhaul in 2005 when it switched to the “Berliner” format. It spent £80m to build specially commissioned new printing presses but later ditched the format and went for tabloid sizing.

data.title: The Cornish mine that could help Trump in his new arms race with China
data.description:
Britain’s last operational tin mine, South Crofty, has been sitting dormant in Cornwall for nearly 30 years. But now the historic site may be revived thanks to an unlikely benefactor: Donald Trump.

The US president’s administration last week said it was willing to loan up to $225m (£165m) to developer Cornish Metals to support the mine’s reopening – so long as it agrees to send some of the output across the Atlantic.

Just two days earlier, and more than 4,600 miles away, a Washington-backed investment fund had announced a deal to acquire a 40pc share in a copper and cobalt mine run by Glencore in the Democratic Republic of Congo.

And in Western Australia, the US government has emerged as one of the backers of a scheme to build a new refinery that will produce gallium, a rare metal.

The investments follow a spate of dealmaking by the Trump administration at home in the past year – which has seen it take stakes in a magnet producer, a lithium mine and rare earth refineries – among other things.

According to experts, they are all examples of the new, more muscular approach Washington is taking to securing critical minerals abroad as it seeks to break China’s stranglehold over global supplies.

Tin, copper, cobalt and gallium are all commonly used in electronics, while lithium – known as “white gold” – is the main ingredient in batteries that power the modern world.

Rare earth magnets, meanwhile, are found in the spinning parts of wind turbines, electric motors and even fighter plane wing flaps.

Without them, and a plethora of other metals, factory production lines across America would grind to a halt.

That briefly became a real prospect last year, when the US was locked in a trade standoff with China.

“One of the challenges we’ve had in the last 20 years in the United States is our mineral security efforts have largely been contained to handshake diplomacy,” says Gracelin Baskaran, a critical minerals expert at the Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS).

“But handshake diplomacy doesn’t bring projects online. And it doesn’t yield minerals that can meet critical security interests.

“It’s the start, but what is the support that we need to get projects into production? It’s financing, it’s trade agreements, it’s off-take agreements [where a customer agrees to buy raw material before it is produced]. That is how we increase access to minerals.

“We’re going from paper agreements to deploying capital.”

US trailing China

This is the thinking behind the dizzying series of deals announced by American officials in recent months.

Efforts to start directly nurturing alternative supplies began under Joe Biden but have accelerated under Trump, with investments made through several arms of the state – including the department of war, the department of energy, the Export-Import Bank of the United States (Exim) and the US International Development Finance Corporation (DFC).

The DFC was the investor that supported Glencore’s copper and cobalt operations, while Exim has extended the offer of loans and financing to the tin project being developed by Cornish Metals.

Yet the Trump administration is still playing catch-up.

Chinese companies have been steadily building their control over key mining and refining operations for decades, giving Beijing the ability to turn off supplies to the rest of the world at will.

Take just one example: copper, the world’s most important industrial metal, used in electrical wiring, circuit boards and plumbing. Analysts predict the world is headed for a supply crunch in the coming years – and China is far better placed to weather it.

The Middle Kingdom’s companies control around 16pc of global copper production compared to 6pc owned by American firms, according to data from Benchmark Mineral Intelligence, and there have been calls in China to boost domestic stockpiles.

“China has been doing this for a while, so this isn’t new for them,” says Albert Mackenzie, an analyst at Benchmark.

“The US may have looked at how they have built up their copper assets overseas, and is now trying to replicate that or not get left behind.

“There’s a big desire for more copper at the moment – you can point at AI, or to the energy transition, but actually it’s in basically everything we use because it’s such a good conductor.”

Minerals monopoly

While China has a grip on supply, its dominance of critical minerals is really about processing, with its share standing at over 50pc for a whole host of metals and exceeding 90pc for certain products.

This means that even though such metals may be mined in the US, Australia or elsewhere, they are still sent to China for refining.

Some 91pc of rare earth magnet metal refining and 94pc of global magnet production is concentrated in China, according to the International Energy Agency.

These are the magnets used in critical parts of the US military’s F-35 stealth fighter, as well as Tomahawk cruise missiles, navy destroyers and submarines, the Stinger portable missile and the M1 Abrams tank.

This leverage was brought into painful focus for Mr Trump last year, when he was forced to walk back his threats of trade tariffs after Beijing imposed export restrictions on heavy rare earths.

Ahead of his visit to China in April, the US president has now approved the creation of a $12bn critical mineral reserve, known as Project Vault, that will seek to guard US manufacturers against similar threats in future.

The initiative has been backed by captains of industry including the bosses of GE Vernova and Boeing.

“We don’t want to ever go through what we went through a year ago,” Mr Trump told reporters last week.

“Just as we have long had a strategic petroleum reserve and a stockpile of critical minerals for national defence, we’re now creating this reserve for American industry, so we don’t have any problems.”

Security and stability

The US administration has also been seeking to rally allies around an international network for developing critical mineral supplies outside of China.

Last week, vice-president JD Vance and Marco Rubio, the secretary of state, hosted a critical minerals summit in Washington aimed at drumming up support for the initiative.

In a speech, Mr Rubio pointed out that the Chinese grip over supplies posed the same danger to all of America’s allies and urged them to work together.

“No country in the world has every critical mineral that’s valuable,” he said. “So it will rely on international cooperation.

“Right now, you have 90-something per cent of some of these elements controlled by one nation, one place. And that’s just not healthy for the global economy and it’s certainly not healthy for security and stability.”

Along with the deals that have been announced, the White House has also vowed to back minerals producers with grants, loans and even “price floors” that will ensure they get paid a minimum amount for their minerals.

This is seen as vital because China’s producers receive large amounts of state support and can otherwise undercut competition from private Western firms.

Dominic Raab, the former Conservative deputy prime minister who now works with minerals investor Appian Capital Advisory, was in the room and says this change in mindset is key.

“There’s a recognition that it’s not a free market, it’s a broken market,” he says. “So they’re going to have to engage at a state level, but they also want to unlock private sector investment, and that’s important for de-risking those investments and unlocking more capital.”

Sophia Gaston, a geopolitics researcher at King’s College London, says the showdown with Beijing last year left Washington officials chastened by America’s “profound vulnerabilities on these supply chains”.

“China called the Trump administration’s bluff, and that forced America back to the negotiation table, and I think they felt extremely exposed by that, and wanted to move quickly to try and prevent that kind of situation happening again,” she says.

“There’s been a very intense flurry of activity, because Donald Trump is pencilled in for a visit to Beijing in April. So I think he wants to feel he’s going into that conversation with the maximum leverage.

“Until that critical minerals and rare earths diversification strategy starts to come into view, that’s going to be difficult – because there will always be a feeling that China can turn off the tap.”

data.title: Bring back the great British TV drama of whisky-swilling businessmen
data.description:
The way we view television has changed enormously in the 73 years since Queen Elizabeth II’s coronation. We’ve gone from flickering black-and-white boxes that took a good 30 seconds to warm up and served up just one channel, to widescreen OLED smart devices that stream thousands of choices of “content” (though, ironically, they still often take a good 30 seconds to start up).

But the actual shows? Although they look very different, the genres of TV drama have stayed remarkably steady over all that time. Flick through the TV listings from any year over the decades, and you will find crime shows, medical shows, soaps, sci-fi, costume drama, historical series, and so on. There’s not all that great a gap between Emergency Ward 10 and Casualty. There’s only one kind of drama series that you will find in a 1966 edition of TV Times that you won’t see while scrolling through your EPG in 2026 (excluding westerns, which were obviously never a British concern anyway). I’m talking about business TV.

There used to be many of these series: The Brothers, The Power Game, The Plane Makers, Mogul, The Troubleshooters. These were gripping stories of men (and it was overwhelmingly men) striding into each others’ offices, pouring whiskies, sparking up cigars, and obsessing over boards, stocks and shares, new products and processes. Women did feature, but until the Eighties they were the wives and secretaries that orbited like satellites around the boardrooms and factory floors where the main events played out.

Now, I will hear you object that boardroom business drama survives – Succession or Industry, for example. The big difference between these modern shows and their forebears is that they aren’t really concerned with the actual business of the business, except where it directly affects the drama between the characters.

Succession didn’t have a storyline about the design of a product – you can’t imagine scenes with Shiv or Roman unfurling plans across a big teak table, or lengthy discussions about the pros and cons of the fuel injection nozzle or whatever. No, Succession is more the inheritor of the Dallas model, the high-glamour soap in which the actual work in hand is merely the convenient backdrop.

The true business TV drama of old portrayed the actual business as dramatic in itself. The heyday of the form was 1963’s The Plane Makers, the story of an aviation design firm which after three series reinvented itself in 1965 as The Power Game, shifting its anti-hero John Wilder (Patrick Wymark) to a merchant bank. The tensions of manufacture, negotiations and industrial relations were depicted in detail that seems baffling today.

There is a sense of great public interest in business in other shows of the time. Early Honor Blackman episodes of The Avengers feature Steed and Mrs Gale investigating chicanery at works making pottery and optical lenses, and trying to expose export licences for counterfeit packaging. It’s all very unusual to 21st-century eyes.

The Wymark model – the tycoon stomping about, all blood and thunder, in his executive suite – became such an established part of TV that Fry and Laurie would spoof it in their John and Peter sketches: “Damn it to damnation, triple blast, John, I want to know what the hell is going on!”

The sometimes strained and phoney scene-setting dialogue of the genre – as we pick up the characters before the meat of the scene – was also brilliantly lampooned by Victoria Wood, who opens a visit to Acorn Antiques with Miss Babs pointing at blank sheets of paper and telling Miss Berta, “Yes, that’s much better – if we deliver these antiques on Friday morning, we can take delivery of these antiques in the afternoon.”

By the Seventies, the genre had become so established that it started generating oddities of its own. Hine, from 1971, is a fascinatingly weird one-off, created by Power Game writer Wilfred Greatorex (what a fantastic “Dammit, Marjorie!” name that is).

Hine starred the personable Barrie Ingham as the titular hero, who is a freelance international arms dealer. Viewers were baffled – were we really meant to be rooting for this charming chap in his attempts to sell British weapons to dictatorships in the Middle East? – and the show flopped. It stands now as a fascinatingly amoral glimpse into a hidden world.

The episode “The Old School Noose” by Arden Winch (another great moniker and nothing to do with the workwear firm of the same name), in which Hine is caught in an elaborate web involving his childhood best friend who is now a Labour MP, is one of those exquisite pieces of dramatic writing and acting that occasionally startles you in a vintage “hack-for-hire” series.

So what became of the “pure” business drama? The last British example I can think of that still retained elements of it was Eighties super-soap Howards’ Way, the BBC’s answer to Dynasty. Much of the action in its initial stages revolved around the design and production of a new racing boat, and the dilemma between using hand-crafted or machine-made mouldings. Try pitching that to Netflix. But fairly soon this faded into the background, swallowed up by the ins and outs of Relton Marine, Leisurecruise, the House of Howard, and which character owned what percentage of them.

In fact, in the Nineties, business started infecting our less glam soaps. When I worked as a writer on Coronation Street, Fiona Middleton – aged 21! – owned the salon, while Brookside’s teenage entrepreneur Jacqui Dixon had fingers in investment pies everywhere, and in EastEnders Sharon Watts became Walford’s answer to Howard Hughes. These may have been intended as inspiring messages of female empowerment, but they were both highly unlikely and difficult for viewers to identify with.

The simple answer to the mystery of business TV’s decline is that Britain stopped manufacturing things. There are currently politicians on all sides mooting the return of long-defunct industries to our land. In the unlikely event that they succeed, perhaps once again viewers will see people slamming down tumblers of Scotch and bellowing at their secretaries, damn it!

data.title: Ocado’s dream of being the Tesla of groceries is unravelling fast
data.description:
When Tim Steiner retires, he would no doubt like to be remembered as Britain’s Elon Musk. After all, it was only recently that the Ocado founder was still breezily comparing the grocery outfit to Tesla.

There is no doubting Steiner’s entrepreneurial vision. But sadly, he increasingly risks being remembered as little more than a very good salesman.

Over the last 25 years, the founder of Ocado has repeatedly convinced some of the supposedly smartest people in the City and on Wall Street that his company is on the cusp of changing the face of supermarket shopping for good.

He has repeated this feat time and time again – persuading shareholders, bankers and the bond markets to lend Ocado vast sums of money to fulfil his dream of bringing online groceries to the masses.

If there was some sign of this coming true, you could perhaps understand their eagerness.

Since its inception, it is estimated that Ocado has raised more than £5.5bn – nearly three times its current stock-market value – from the equity and debt markets. Yet, when you consider that this is a business that has racked up £1.8bn of losses since going public in 2010, the enthusiasm is baffling

Perhaps the news that Ocado is planning as many as 1,000 job cuts as part of a scramble to rein in costs will convince the die-hards that Steiner’s “jam-tomorrow” story is destined to remain so forever.

The company has said little in response to reports of the cuts, other than that it regularly reviews its operations “to ensure we’re set up for long-term success,” – which is just silly. Ditto the idea that firing hundreds of people is somehow a demonstration of strength.

Instead, it smacks of an organisation running out of ideas and, potentially road. The truth is Ocado is in another spin, possibly its biggest yet, after several major customers watered down their ties with the Hatfield-based company.

Against that grim backdrop, all eyes are on Ocado’s annual results in a fortnight’s time after a 40pc slump in its share price in the last six months.

For Steiner to retain whatever credibility he has left after the disastrous events of recent months, the company needs to honour its promise to turn cash flow positive in the next financial year.

Its evangelical founder has said this is Ocado’s “core priority”, and full-year profitability could come shortly.

Yet with key customers scaling back their partnerships, it is undeniable that there is suddenly a sharper focus on what insiders describe as a commitment to “rigorous cost and capital discipline”.

Bernstein analyst William Woods predicted last month that there will now need to be “material cost-cutting” to the workforce, as well as the capital to invest in its supposedly world-beating technology.

At the same time, he pared his target share price to 100p, which is 58pc below the 229p level at which the stock closed on Monday.

Steiner’s grand vision has long been for Ocado to evolve from an online grocery delivery service to a technology provider that builds everything from consumer-facing websites to robot-operated depots for other food retailers – “the Tesla of grocery”, as he called it as recently as 2023.

It’s an intoxicating story that goes a long way to explaining why sophisticated institutional shareholders such as the hedge fund Roditi and money giant Schroders have been backers from day one.

However, the inconvenient truth that Steiner and his acolytes continue to overlook is that grocery deliveries remain unprofitable.

This is particularly true of the sort of hi-tech wizardry that Ocado provides.

The biggest problem facing the company is the vast amounts of upfront capital required to roll out large, robot-operated warehouses, the accompanying software and fleets of refrigerated vans – hence the need to repeatedly raise large sums of money from investors and the debt markets.

It is a model built on the premise that such outlays would eventually be recovered through lucrative long-term contracts with the biggest supermarkets worldwide, who would be happy to pay hefty licensing and consultancy fees for the privilege of using Ocado’s state-of-the-art technology.

For a while, it seemed a plausible scenario. Never more so than during the pandemic, when Steiner declared that “a dramatic and permanent shift towards online grocery shopping” was underway.

It was a boast that helped propel Ocado’s share price to the nosebleed heights of £29, and at one stage left the company worth more than double Sainsbury’s and Morrisons combined.

For now, management can still point to a long list of major customers, including Coles in Australia, Casino Group in France and ICA in Sweden. But for how long?

Kroger’s bombshell decision in November to close three of eight Ocado warehouses in the US was followed in January by an announcement from Canada’s Sobeys that it would close a distribution centre using Ocado’s kit.

The Kroger pullback on its own threatens to be pretty devastating to Ocado’s prospects.

The US chain is its most valuable customer by a long way – “not just half the embedded value, probably half the future value” – in the words of one retail chief. And its claim that the Ocado set-up only works in “higher-density” areas suggests serious limitations to the expansionary story it has been selling for years.

Ever the optimist, Steiner has attempted to spin the setback as an opportunity.

Under the Kroger tie-up, Ocado was prevented from doing deals with its rivals in the US and other international markets. Therefore, it is now free to seek other partners, the logic goes.

But with Sobeys quickly struck by buyer’s remorse too, surely the overwhelming question isn’t how many new customers Ocado can bring in but how many of its existing customers it can hold onto?

Steiner may be putting on a brave face, but a warning from Kroger that it will be keeping tabs on results at the remaining Ocado facilities must be giving him sleepless nights.

If his biggest customer is struggling to make the economics work, then surely the rest are?
