data.title: The towns where the taps ran dry
data.description:
On a grey Tuesday in East Grinstead, a little girl runs along the pavement to the redbrick town library. She stands at the automatic glass doors waiting for them to open, but nothing happens. A moment later her mother arrives. The woman looks at the sign that reads “library closed today”, then tells the child she will not be able to borrow any books after all.

Like almost every business or public building in this West Sussex town, the library is shut as a result of South East Water’s failure to keep the taps running.

The company’s bosses might just about be forgiven if this were a one-off. But it is the second officially declared major incident in South East Water territory in as many months, with customers in Tunbridge Wells, Kent, still facing disruption after weeks of problems.

South East has repeatedly fallen short on its most basic duty. In 2023, 6,000 households in Kent and Sussex went without water when supplies at South East ran out. In 2022, thousands of people spent Christmas with no water due to leakages.

The company, which serves 2.3 million customers, is now well on its way to claiming the crown of Britain’s worst utility from basket-case Thames Water, leaving its licence to operate under threat.

Businesses on the brink

Dave Hinton, the chief executive of South East Water, who took home £515,000 last year including a £115,000 bonus, was hauled in front of a parliamentary committee earlier this month. He had been summoned to answer questions on the crisis that hit Tunbridge Wells in December and ruined Christmas for 24,000 homes and businesses.

The spa town made an unlikely backdrop for a water crisis. From the 17th century, Tunbridge Wells’s mineral waters drew bathers and wealth.

But in the 21st century, water dealt an economic blow. Pub and bar owners in Tunbridge Wells told The Telegraph they had lost ruinous amounts of money.

“In four days we lost between £15,000 to £20,000. We are really struggling,” Bianca Omer, the manager of the cocktail bar Rocca said.

The bar survived. But Tudor Price, the chief executive of Kent’s chamber of commerce, believes that pubs, restaurants and bars in Tunbridge Wells “potentially lost about £20m of revenue” as a result of the outage.

Some are now on the brink. With many pubs already threatened by the Chancellor’s business rates raid, lengthy closure may deliver the final blow for some in Tunbridge Wells.

More alarmingly, a kidney treatment centre, 19 care homes, and two hospitals were left without water too.

‘Flying blind’

Debate has raged over whether the crisis in Tunbridge Wells was foreseeable. Hinton, perhaps unsurprisingly, reckons not.

At his parliamentary hearing he told Alistair Carmichael, the no-nonsense MP for Orkney and chairman of the environment select committee, that the whole thing was a bolt from the blue. In fact, because the crisis came as such a shock, Hinton insisted, he’d give himself “eight out of 10 in terms of response”.

The A-grade mark, as Carmichael put it, raised eyebrows among committee members. But when Marcus Rink, the boss of the Drinking Water Inspectorate, delivered his verdict, the benefit of the doubt evaporated.

“It shouldn’t have been a surprise,” Rink said. The water treatment works at nearby Pembury hadn’t just gone down for a few hours – it had been failing for some time, he explained.

“The start of the incident, in the opinion of my inspectors, was Nov 9.” With the Pembury plant the only water treatment works serving Tunbridge Wells, its continued good health is essential to the area.

Rink said South East was “flying blind”. The plant lacked electronic monitoring, relying on manual observations that missed failing filters.

Despite prior regulatory enforcement notices and missing micro-filtration units, the company had ignored weeks of visible deterioration, eventually leading to a totally avoidable system collapse.

The picture for South East became worse as Rink laid out more details. The company had been notified that there were issues with the treatment works a year previously. Testing had been intermittent, and the specialists monitoring the facility had all knocked off on Friday afternoon before returning on Monday morning. Nov 9 was a Sunday.

If MPs have been determined to hold Hinton to account, the same cannot be said of South East Water’s non-executive directors. Other managers have been sent out to face the music, among them a flustered Neil Hudson, South East’s head of water quality.

“What would happen to people if they do drink it?” a local BBC news reporter demanded to know after residents were advised to boil their water before using it.

“Any sort of normal, healthy individual will probably see no impact whatsoever. If anyone drinks the water and starts to feel unwell, our advice would be to speak to your GP,” Hudson said.

Asked by MPs why he had dodged the media, Hinton claimed journalists would not have asked relevant questions. They would have focused on his salary and South East Water’s finances, he complained.

When he finally came out of hiding, Hinton told ITV that he was “an operational CEO” and as such had spent the crisis participating in “traditional CEO activities”.

Henry Tuffnell, the young Labour MP for Pembrokeshire who sits on the environment committee, pointed out that for an “operational CEO” Hinton seemed to be remarkably short of operational detail.

Tuffnell tells The Telegraph: “You can’t say you were all over the detail, and therefore didn’t have time to do any media, if you weren’t over the detail.”

Even as Hinton faced MPs, back in Tunbridge Wells a second major incident was already in the offing. Still recovering from the pre-Christmas outage, locals were cut off again on Saturday.

Round two, according to South East, was due to “recent cold weather and a subsequent breakout of leaks and bursts”. This was no minor blip. Within days, 30,000 homes and businesses across Kent and Sussex had no water at all.

‘We have had enough’

In East Grinstead, four schoolboys kick a yellow football against the wall. They are the only people in the town who seem to think the outage is a good thing. “It’s a day off,” one of them offers cheerfully.

In Waitrose, almost all of the water has gone from the shelves except for a few bottles of San Pellegrino and some strawberry-flavoured Volvic. There was a water station in the car park the previous day, but it closed after 90 minutes.

East Grinstead council said: “We were not contacted directly by South East Water at any stage during the recent water supply disruption in the town. To the best of the council’s understanding, local knowledge was not utilised when setting up the initial water distribution stations.

“The town council regrets that it was not involved from the outset, as early engagement and local knowledge could have helped ensure a smoother, safer, and more coordinated response for the town’s residents.”

Local knowledge is clearly not South East Water’s strong suit. In December the company began its comedy of errors in Tunbridge Wells with the opening of a water station in Tonbridge, seven miles away.

For those affected, it’s hard to see the funny side. Gary, who lives homeless in East Grinstead, and had just two bottles of water left, told The Telegraph he was “scared”. Like many in the town, he wondered: “What is this all about?”

Jonathan Hawker, a Tunbridge Wells resident who set up the campaign group Dry Wells Action, says: “We can’t wash, flush toilets, do the dishes, wash clothes, top up boilers – all the normal stuff you take for granted – because of this abject failure by South East Water.

“We have had enough. We will now fight for our right to water. Tunbridge Wells is no longer disgusted. We are absolutely furious.”

Heavy hitters

South East’s board is now facing serious questions. Chris Train, the chairman, has been no more forthcoming about appearing publicly than his chief executive.

Described on the company’s website as bringing “a lot of experience”, he is also chairman of Sellafield, which in 2024 was fined for serious cyber security failings. The Commons public accounts committee criticised the nuclear power plant last year for “failure, cost-overruns and continuing safety concerns”.

After Hinton’s select committee mauling, Train offered a review by one of the non-executive directors.

Committee chairman Alistair Carmichael responded: “Members of the press and public would be forgiven for seeing Mr Train’s announcement of a further review of the Pembury failure as an attempt to buy themselves time, to hunker down until this storm blows over.

“They also assert that their review will be ‘independent’. It stretches the meaning of ‘independent’ when their review is to be conducted by a member of its board, supported by its staff. What was the investigation done by the Drinking Water Inspectorate if it was not an independent review?”

Hinton’s furious phone call

Desperate Tunbridge Wells residents have called for the Army to be brought in. Though that may sound a little over the top, there is logic to the request.

Hawker says: “The Army are logistics specialists who are used to dealing with challenging situations. We need experts who can manage logistics in emergency situations.”

South East Water officials can’t even manage a basic task like carting bottles of water from one place to another, he argues.

If Hinton has been the baddie, Mike Martin, the Liberal Democrat MP for Tunbridge Wells, has emerged as something of a local hero. He has had his sleeves rolled up throughout the process and repeatedly taken Hinton to task. This resulted in Hinton phoning Martin one evening during the eye of the storm to give him both barrels.

In his evidence to the environment committee, Martin said Hinton had accused him of politicising the crisis, asking: “How can you sleep at night?” The chief executive admitted to Carmichael that the telephone tantrum had not been his “finest moment”.

South East is more used to getting away with it.

As well as the four major shortages customers have endured on Hinton’s watch, South East Water has ranked in the bottom three companies for disruptions every year since Hinton took over. In 2023, it had the dubious accolade of recording the most interruptions and outages in England and Wales.

Its performance is getting worse, not better. In 2025, just 13 of it 38 annual targets were met – a little more than a third. Among the areas where it fell short were leakages, supply interruptions, water taste and odour, customer satisfaction and value for money. The previous two years, South East scored 15 – equivalent to less than 40pc. In 2022, it ranked positively on 24 of the 38 goals.

The company has suggested that its systems would be much better if only it had been able to raise customers’ bills, a familiar refrain in the water industry.

Yet it already has. Bills went up by 20pc just last year, meaning the average customer now pays £294 a year. It is also one of only five companies pushing to lift bills above the figure set by the regulator over the next five years.

Ofwat also gave short shrift to South East’s most recent investment plan. The company’s performance commitments were “of low to moderate ambition and do not go far enough in some cases”, the regulator said last April.

When it comes to “water supply interruptions, per capita consumption, repairs to burst mains and biodiversity”, the targets South East Water proposed “remained among the lowest across the sector”, Ofwat added.

South East responded by accusing the watchdog of being “unfair, disproportionate and punitive”. The company boasts of having “60 teams repairing leaks reactively and 86 leakage technicians and analysts searching for and finding leaks proactively”. More than 25,000 leaks were repaired in 2024, it says.

For any customers who feel they’re not getting value for money, the company points out: “The average drinking water bill now costs 81p per day, less than the cost of a second-class stamp.”

A spokesman said: “Our priority is improving South East Water in the future. We are strategically focused on our business plan and committed to delivering the infrastructure investment needed. This will improve our resilience as we face the challenges of climate change and population growth.”

It adds that Pembury’s water treatment works at the centre of the Tunbridge Wells crisis is due a full upgrade: “In advance of this full refurbishment, a number of improvements and changes are being made. Some of these are complete, and others are still ongoing, all of which are in line with the regulators’ timescales.”

Finances are underwater

South East’s financial performance is no less wretched than its operational performance. The company has failed to post a profit for the last three years, and though pre-tax losses roughly halved to £19.7m last year, they total £130m since 2022.

Weighed down with £1.3bn of borrowings – a figure that has steadily increased every year under Hinton – the cost of servicing those debts regularly eclipses the company’s profits. In the last five years, more than £400m has gone out of the door on debt costs and finance charges alone.

A further £168m has been paid out dividends, £136m of it in the form of a regulator-approved repayment of an internal loan to its parent company in 2020. South East Water is ultimately owned by a consortium comprising Australian and Canadian funds and NatWest’s pension fund.

In the two-year period from 2020 to 2022, the amount that South East paid out in dividends and debt interest surpassed what it spent upgrading infrastructure.

In 2024, the company revealed that it needed further cash to stay afloat. Ten months later, it received a £200m equity injection from shareholders.

Yet none of this has stopped Hinton receiving hundreds of thousands of pounds in pay and bonuses every year. Total earnings since assuming the top job in 2020 stand at an estimated £2.3m. Last year, he was paid a basic salary of £307,000, a £115,000 bonus and received a further £35,000 in pension contributions and other benefits.

A spokesman for South East said: “South East Water remains committed to a remuneration framework that supports a performance culture, and recognises success but does not reward poor performance.

“The bonus scheme is evaluated by the company’s independent non-executive director-led remuneration committee. The executive directors are not part of this committee and have no say in their remuneration.

“We can confirm that no bonus payment was made for operational performance for the year 2024-25. Details of all payments made can be found in our annual report on our website.”

‘Out of control’

Yet pinning the fiasco on a lack of investment when executives’ backs are against the wall doesn’t wash with Tunbridge Wells MP Mike Martin.

“Quite clearly, corporate governance has broken down,” he said. “We are now in a situation where a major public utility is out of control – failing its customers, risking public health and treating Parliament with contempt.”

If the board won’t step up, Martin thinks the utility’s bosses have to get involved. NatWest’s pension scheme owns 25pc of South East Water.

“NatWest Group Pension Fund will use its influence as a minority shareholder to direct South East Water’s board to ensure these issues are fully resolved,” it said in response to a letter from Martin this week.

The regulator has been spurred to action too. On Wednesday evening, the Government finally showed some mettle by calling on Ofwat to review South East Water’s licence to operate.

In an uncharacteristic show of speediness, on Thursday Ofwat announced an investigation into whether the supplier had met its obligations and offered support to those affected. Ofwat sceptics point out the regulator has been investigating South East since 2023.

There are local suspicions that Labour have, to a degree, let the crisis escalate because Tunbridge Wells and East Grinstead will never turn red. Richard Tice, the deputy leader of Reform UK, told The Telegraph that’s overthinking it. He said: “The problem is utter management incompetence at South East Water. My hunch is cock-up, not conspiracy.”

Yet the crisis is ongoing, and the inquisition is just getting started. By the water station at East Grinstead sports club, where the cars are backed up as far as you can see and people are pushing shopping trolleys full of bottles back home, a little boy on a bike asks if you can drink the water from the pond.

data.title: Debt-laden Very Group secures £150m breathing space for sale
data.description:
The lender that seized control of Very from the Barclay family has provided the online retailer with £150m in financial support while it attempts to engineer a sale.

US investment giant Carlyle has pumped new money into Very as part of a support package to ease financial pressures on the debt-laden business.

As well as providing a cash injection, Carlyle has also converted some of its debt into equity to help reduce pressure on the retailer from interest payments.

The Telegraph understands that Carlyle’s total package is £150m.

The support is part of a broader refinancing, which has been carried out as Carlyle tries to sell the company for £2bn.

Bankers at Barclays and JPMorgan have been tasked with selling the business after Carlyle took control of Very in November 2025, ending the Barclay family’s involvement in the retailer after two decades.

Before that, Carlyle had been the retailer’s main corporate lender.

International Media Investments (IMI), the Abu Dhabi media vehicle that was involved in a failed effort to acquire The Telegraph from the Barclay family, also became a major lender to Very in that complex transaction.

Both Carlyle and IMI helped to keep the Liverpool-based business afloat as the Barclays’ business empire unravelled.

Carlyle is now seeking to put the company, which sells a wide range of clothes, toys and electrical goods, on a more stable footing ahead of a potential sale.

In addition to its own £150m support package, which is aimed at reducing debts, Very is also understood to have secured more breathing space from other external lenders. Bosses are expected to announce details of the financing on Monday.

The business has extended a £150m credit facility for another three years until February 2030, while also prolonging a £1.8bn “securitisation facility” until February 2029.

Otherwise known as consumer credit, this £1.8bn pot provided by a consortium of banks was set to expire next year.

Very has also secured better terms on some of its borrowings, which were a total of £2.3bn last year, according to company filings. That is compared with revenues of £2bn from 4.4 million customers.

Robbie Feather, chief executive of Very, hailed the company’s strong trading performance over Christmas when sales grew 1.9pc in the six weeks to Dec 27.

The latest refinancing follows a prolonged period of uncertainty for Very, which was caught up in the crisis engulfing the Barclays.

The family had launched an aborted attempt to sell the business in early 2025 before Carlyle seized control in November.

The change in ownership led to Very posting losses of £500m in 2025 after it was forced to write off a major loan to its former owners. This brought an end to the family’s involvement in the business.

Very, which was once viewed as the jewel in the crown of the Barclays’ empire, was formed 20 years ago through the merger of Littlewoods and Shop Direct, overseen by Sir Frederick Barclay and the late Sir David Barclay.

Aidan Barclay, the eldest of Sir David’s four sons, chaired the business until May 2024, when he was replaced by Nadhim Zahawi.

As well as losing control of Very, the Barclays have also lost a string of other prized assets in recent years, including The Telegraph.

Very declined to comment.

data.title: Eurostar to cut boarding times by 30 minutes
data.description:
Eurostar plans to cut boarding times by 30 minutes after bosses admitted that St Pancras International station had become too much like an airport.

Under the planned shake-up, St Pancras passengers will be able to board Eurostar trains straight away rather than having to wait in the departures hall.

Passengers are currently required to check in 60 minutes before boarding, but the new system means they will be permitted to board up to half an hour before their train departs.

The move will allow people to turn up much closer to the time that their train is due to leave, easing pressure on the station as Eurostar expands and as rival rail companies launch services through the Channel Tunnel.

Wendy Spinks, the chief commercial officer at London St Pancras Highspeed, said the terminus needed to function more like a domestic railway station. She warned that taking the Eurostar had become too much like flying.

She said: “It cannot be the equivalent of an airport departure lounge. We see it being a really quick process. Going straight to the train is part of the plan.

“It has become too close to the airport experience, where you check in, go to security, wait in the lounge and then rush to the gate.

“You will always want some people to turn up a bit earlier so that you can process them and get them on board but at least 50pc of passengers should go straight to the train.”

The changes to speed up wait times will be rolled out with the remodelling of St Pancras as it prepares for the launch of services by Sir Richard Branson’s Virgin Trains and Trenitalia of Italy by the end of the decade.

Ms Spinks said the departures logjam acted as the main obstacle to growth, with the tunnel and five international platforms at St Pancras already able to take many more trains.

Overcrowding worsened with the expansion of border controls at the station including the installation of kiosks to handle biometric checks for the EU’s entry and exit system.

She said: “Over time, security and border processing has taken up more and more space so that when you get through security, there’s nothing there.

“There is a mismatch between what the route can handle and what the station can handle.”

The £100m remodelling is expected to free up space by expanding the queuing area into the barely used arrivals hall adjoining the departures area.

Platforms will be shared between operators, with passengers expected to find their own way to the correct train.

She said: “If you look at Gare du Nord in Paris, they’ve got trains going all over the place. It’s your job to get on the right one.”

Ms Spinks said reducing the waiting time at St Pancras was also seen as key to luring more people on to the train over longer distances.

She said travelling by train “should be less stressful”, adding: “You can work and sit at a table as a group and you get to the centre of a city rather than a distant airport.

“But the more you go beyond two hours and start getting out to Germany or Geneva, say, then your time differential is harder against flying. If we shrink the time that you hang around in the station it cuts the end-to-end journey.”

Ms Spinks said that St Pancras Highspeed, which also owns the HS1 line to the Channel Tunnel, is considering applications from potential new entrants and that it expects to make recommendations on access to the Office of Rail and Road in the next few weeks.

She said there could be no guarantee that all parties will be granted the volume of services that they are seeking.

data.title: Labour’s property market plot could hammer your house price
data.description:
When Harold Wilson launched Labour’s housebuilding blitz in 1964, he hoped to tap into Britain’s wartime spirit of two decades earlier to unleash a British building renaissance.

“Just as in the war, the idea of a common purpose had a dynamising effect, so possibly the launching of a great housing plan could have a similar effect today,” he told Labour members.

Wilson’s Labour government went on to phenomenal success, building over 400,000 homes a year by 1968 – a UK record which is still to be surpassed.

Almost 60 years on and Labour’s latest crop of ministers are wrestling with a similar housing crisis.

They have pledged to build 300,000 homes a year while in power.

But instead of Wilson’s soaring rhetoric, ministers are pushing a more puzzling message to galvanise the public: to help more young people get on the property ladder, we are going to hammer the price of your house to help make things fairer.

Last week, Matthew Pennycook, the housing minister, admitted that house prices will fall if Labour meets its target to build 1.5 million homes this parliament.

“Our analysis, in broad terms, estimates that a 1pc increase in housing stock should … decrease prices by 2pc,” Pennycook told a Commons committee on Tuesday.

Pennycook, a 43-year-old former think tank academic from west London, said more housebuilding would lead to a “sustained levelling-off of house price growth, and then a gradual easing of it over time”.

It is an aim that may resonate with young people locked out of the property market.

But for swathes of British homeowners, it has sparked fears that Labour is now coming for their property nest eggs.

Property is an obsession in this country because it comprises 40pc of household wealth in the UK – with much of that going towards funding retirement.

“People who paid off their mortgages and have got an awful lot of equity tied up in their housing are protective of the price of their houses,” says Paul Cheshire, a professor at the London School of Economics.

“There is a tension between what you might call the housing ‘haves’ and the housing ‘have-nots’. The balance is moving towards the housing have-nots.”

In a bid to calm fears, Steve Reed, the Housing Secretary, was rushed out after Pennycook’s appearance to dispel the idea that Labour wanted to hurt house prices.

“We don’t want house prices to come down where people own their own home,” he said.

“We need to build more homes so it’s more affordable for young people in particular to get that first foot on the housing ladder.”

Yet the alarming messaging around the plan has prompted further scrutiny on Labour’s building policy.

The party came to power in 2024 promising a housing revolution.

Guided by Angela Rayner, the former deputy prime minister, it said 1.5 million homes would be built by 2030 – about 300,000 a year.

Today, Labour claims it has started 310,000 homes since it came into power, equalling an average monthly rate of 17,200.

This leaves 1.2 million to finish in the remaining 42 months until July 2029 – meaning they need to build 28,000 per month.

Yet, according to Tom Bill, head of UK residential research at Knight Frank, attempts to bring down house prices by increasing supply “seems like a simple economic truth but [it] fundamentally misunderstands how the sector operates”.

“Housebuilders deliver homes at a rate and price that is sustainable for their business, not because it’s written down in a political manifesto,” he says.

“The whole development process needs to be smoother and affordability is a genuine concern in some areas, but this latest intervention is like asking a car manufacturer to speed up its production line in order to sell cheaper hatchbacks,” Bill adds.

Britain has faced a severe shortage of housing for decades. Since 2000, house prices have risen by nearly 250pc, locking many out of the market and forcing them to rent or live under their parents’ roofs.

For Pennycook, much of the blame for this is on the housebuilders and the property industry.

“People in London that bought land at the top of the market for too high a price and are now sitting on it. And there will probably need to be a market adjustment in London,” he told the Financial Times last month.

Tim Leunig, a former adviser to Rishi Sunak, called Pennycook’s objective a “sensible aspiration” but doubted Labour could make it stick.

“If prices fell by 1pc or 2pc a year for a while that would be great,” he said. “Add in inflation … and real wage growth … and you are looking at a 5pc improvement in affordability a year, or close to 25pc in a parliament.

“That would be transformational for a lot of people. Sadly [Labour’s] policies are unlikely to deliver that.”

Builders have also disputed Pennycook’s diagnosis of the problem, saying his claims are “bonkers”.

Graham Prothero, chief executive of housebuilder MJ Gleeson, says: “There appears to be a bit of an ideology that says, if developers didn’t ask for such high profits, then houses would be cheap and everything would be wonderful. It cannot be that simple.”

He adds that he was “very disappointed” at an implication that prices are high because developers sit on land so that it can gain more value over time.

“If my business is to make money out of selling houses, why on earth would I hold them back on the basis that I might make speculative profit out of a green field?” says Prothero.

“It is absolutely bonkers. The moment I buy that site, it’s costing me money. What I want to do is get it through planning and sell the houses, then I can start to close off my risk and actually hopefully sell [at] a profit.”

Politically, the mixed signals coming from the housing department also underscore an ideological divide between the different factions of the Labour Party.

With the likes of Andy Burnham and Ed Miliband applying more pressure on Sir Keir to lean Left after swerving last week’s leadership challenge, Pennycook’s stance is likely to be interpreted as a way for Sir Keir to score some political points.

Many voters have children who may be stuck at home and need help with funding their deposits for a place of their own.

But it could alienate the share of Labour voters who own properties and have swung towards the party away from the Conservatives in recent years.

In the General Election, Labour secured a 17-point lead among mortgage holders and reduced the Tories’ advantage with outright homeowners from 35 points to just 10 points, an Ipsos poll found.

Labour has largely pinned its housebuilding ambitions on bringing in planning reforms to ease the regulatory burden on property developers.

London, which underpins much of Labour’s housebuilding target, remains a fundamental concern for many in the industry, given it remains hugely expensive relative to people’s incomes.

Housebuilding has slumped by 84pc over the past decade, according to Molior, with construction starting on just 5,547 private homes in 2025.

“In London, we are nowhere near building enough to keep pace with half of what demand is, let alone outpace demand and bring prices down,” says Sam Dumitriu, the head of policy at the think tank Britain Remade.

‘Bad deal’ for builders

Building thousands of new homes every year also has implications if they are marketed incorrectly.

Dumitriu points out that putting every single home on a newly built estate on the market at once would lead to “quite a lot of vacancy” and create potential for negative price impacts.

“You might say that it’s a good deal for consumers, a bad deal for the builder, but fundamentally the builder has to factor that in when they build it,” he says.

Labour and Pennycook are due to set out a new housing strategy in the coming months.

With Labour’s last building blitz in the 1960s a distant memory, Sir Keir and his colleagues will need to find their own wartime spirit to achieve their aims.

A spokesman at the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government said: “We are fixing the housing crisis we inherited by accelerating reforms to get Britain building and restore the dream of home ownership.”

The spokesman added: “Major planning changes are already under way and we’re investing £39bn in social and affordable housing.

“Our landmark plan for new towns across the country will also create more attractive, well-designed places where people want to live.”

data.title: Boohoo tycoon must have known about Leicester sweatshops, lawyers claim
data.description:
The co-founder of Boohoo is likely to have known about the “terrible, unsafe and unsanitary conditions” in Leicester sweatshops that supplied his business before the scandal came to light, a High Court lawsuit has claimed.

Mahmud Kamani, who started the online clothes seller in 2006, was probably aware that the factories Boohoo bought its clothes from were underpaying workers and subjecting them to “arbitrary abuse and humiliation,” lawyers claimed.

“It is not, the claimants say, credible that Mr Kamani was unaware of the conditions in which those factories were being operated,” the lawsuit says.

“Boohoo denies the alleged knowledge of its board. The claimants say that these denials are not convincing.”

The claim emerged in a lawsuit in which investors are seeking £177m plus interest from Boohoo in compensation for a share price crash in 2020.

Boohoo’s stock fell by 42pc after press reports revealed workers at Leicester factories that supply it were paid “significantly less than the minimum wage” with many on as little as £3 per hour. Staff were also working long hours in cramped conditions and dilapidated buildings.

City law firm Fox Williams, representing the investors, claims Mr Kamani, Boohoo’s executive chairman, had long-standing relationships with the owners of the Leicester sweatshops, attended social events with some of the suppliers and even attended some of the factories himself.

Lawyers are now pushing for Mr Kamani to hand over logs of his communications with two of his sons, Umar and Samir, on the basis that those messages likely contain relevant information.

The skeleton argument says Mr Kamani “is highly likely to have discussed relevant matters with his sons,” while noting both Umar and Samir Kamani held positions in the company as the heads of major brands.

Labour rights activists claimed that factory staff were also expected to come into work while sick with Covid-19 at the height of the pandemic, when Leicester was under stringent lockdown restrictions.

Controversy wiped £1bn off value

The controversy wiped more than £1bn off of Boohoo’s value following the plunge in the company’s share price on London’s Alternative Investment Market. Boohoo subsequently launched a £55m takeover of Debenhams in 2021 and rebranded under the British department store’s name.

The lawsuit says Boohoo failed to “take its social responsibility seriously” and “did not strive for high standards in ethics throughout its supply chain,” prior to the scandal being unveiled.

It points out that the wider Kamani family also held 37pc of all shares in Boohoo, while pointing to claims the listed business had “vestiges and traits of a family business.”

It says that the Kamani family had links to some of the Leicester factories and that some of the suppliers were even introduced to Boohoo by Mr Kamani’s brother, Jalal, the company’s former trading director.

In response, lawyers for Boohoo, from Herbert Smith Freehills, said: “There is no reasonable basis to include members of the Kamani family other than Mahmud Kamani,” while noting none of them held senior leadership positions inside the fast-fashion retailer.

Boohoo said it “strongly contests the allegations and will vigorously defend any claim.”

The investors include the California State Teachers’ Retirement System, or CalSTRS, which manages over $390bn (£285bn) worth of pensions.

data.title: Independent takeover of The Standard faces delays after board exodus
data.description:
The Independent’s proposed takeover of The Standard is facing delays after a boardroom clear-out plunged the London newspaper into chaos.

The Telegraph has learnt that Tamar Riley has resigned as chief executive of The Standard after just seven months in the role. Her exit follows the departure of Anna van Praagh, the company’s chief content officer, earlier this week.

The changes have taken place ahead of The Independent’s planned deal to take over the running of The Standard’s website and commercial operations. The deal was announced three weeks ago but completion is now poised to be pushed back.

The transfer was initially planned for March 1, with journalists told they could apply for voluntary redundancy before that date. Anyone who refused to transfer would have their contracts terminated.

However, bosses have acknowledged that the transfer is now unlikely to take place next month as planned.

One senior insider said there were “new levels of chaos” at The Standard, which shut down its daily print operations and moved to a weekly edition in late 2024.

Those inside the newsroom now fear that The Standard’s remaining digital operations could be shut down if the deal falls apart.

A source close to The Independent insisted that the executive departures would not be a barrier to negotiations, nor to the success of the deal.

Staff were already in uproar over plans to combine the two titles, which are both backed by Lord Lebedev.

Despite the takeover, The Standard said it would maintain a “core” team of around 25 people.

The abrupt exit of The Standard’s two most senior executives underscores the recent turmoil at the London freesheet amid declining readership.

The rise of smartphones, flexible working and Wi-Fi on the London Underground have all dented reader numbers and sparked heavy losses, forcing the newspaper to shutter its daily edition.

The restructuring last year led to around 150 job losses including at least 70 cuts in editorial, slashing the newsroom by more than half.

The Standard has also been without a permanent editor since the overhaul, when Dylan Jones stepped down after just 18 months in charge.

Meanwhile, critics have questioned the purpose of the new weekly edition, which is more focused on arts and culture than on London news.

The publisher’s website has also shifted its focus to social media-friendly celebrity news – a change in direction that some fear will only accelerate under The Independent.

Ms Riley took over as chief executive of The Standard in July, having previously led female-focused digital media brand Refinery29.

Ms van Praagh is an experienced journalist who has worked at The Standard for more than a decade.

She was expected to oversee the transition to The Independent but raised eyebrows last month when she launched her own newsletter on Substack.

The Standard has been contacted for comment.

data.title: Morrisons to sell dozens of pharmacies in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Morrisons has put dozens of pharmacies up for sale in a fresh effort to cut costs.

The private equity-owned supermarket has launched a sale process after concluding that many of its in-store pharmacies are not financially viable.

As a result of the review, property agents have been tasked with selling some of the pharmacies on a store-by-store basis, rather than the entire portfolio.

Those affected are likely to remain open in stores, but under the brand of any eventual buyer.

The move to sell dozens of pharmacies comes after Morrisons’ losses plunged to £381m last year, fuelled by steep borrowing costs.

Rising debt pressures have led bosses to reduce borrowings, which fell to £3.1bn from £3.5bn last summer.

The sale of pharmacies is the latest attempt to strip back the business and focus increasingly on traditional retail.

A separate restructuring last year triggered the closure of four pharmacies, alongside 52 cafés, 17 convenience stores, 35 meat counters and 13 florists.

This led to more than 300 jobs being put at risk of redundancy, as bosses identified areas “where the costs of operations are significantly out of line with usage, volumes, or the value that customers place on them”.

Rami Baitieh, chief executive of Morrisons, said the cuts were a “necessary part of our plans to renew and reinvigorate Morrisons”.

Morrisons is pursuing a turnaround strategy after Mr Baitieh said the business had been going through a “challenging period”.

The retail boss blamed weaker consumer spending among poorer shoppers, many of whom have been affected by price rises.

This has led to Morrisons struggling to boost its share of the grocery market, which is at 8.4pc.

That is up narrowly from 8.5pc this time last year, although it is well below the 9.9pc it achieved in October 2021, when it was bought by private equity firm Clayton Dubilier & Rice in a £10bn deal.

This has prompted speculation that Morrisons could lose its position as Britain’s fifth-largest supermarket to Lidl, which now boasts 7.7pc of the grocery market.

Morrisons is not alone in seeking to sell off pharmacies, as the sector has been battered by rising costs in recent years.

Around 1,400 pharmacies have shut in England since 2017, according to the National Pharmacy Association, leaving many households unable to access medicine in their areas.

This has been blamed on rising wage costs and a shortfall in how much the NHS pays pharmacies.

Boots has also closed hundreds of pharmacies in recent years, while LloydsPharmacy, which had 138 sites, collapsed in 2023.

Rachel Reeves has piled more pressure on pharmacies over the past year by increasing business rates and the minimum wage.

Supermarkets have also come under pressure from rising taxes under Labour, most notably by the Chancellor’s decision to increase employer National Insurance rates.

Speaking last month, Mr Baitieh pleaded with the Government to avoid hitting retailers with any extra costs.

He said: “I would say, please, no more. This is very important for the market. We expect inflation to go up.”

Morrisons declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Labour hits ferries with new net zero tax
data.description:
Northern Ireland businesses are set to be hammered by a green tax which bosses fear will make them uncompetitive.

A new tax on ferries to Northern Ireland, set to be introduced this summer, will swell freight rates – weighing on the economy and driving up prices.

The levy system, known as the emissions trading scheme (ETS), stands to increase shipping costs to the province by 6pc when it comes into force in July, according industry estimates.

The UK Chamber of Shipping said that will put more pressure on an economy where supply chain costs are already higher than the rest of the UK because of arrangements imposed under Brexit’s Windsor Framework.

It said the tax will also add to the red tape foisted on business following the agreement, which placed Northern Ireland in the EU’s single market for goods after Brexit.

The ETS scheme requires shipping firms to pay for their CO2 emissions as part of the Government’s push to reach net zero by 2050.

Unlike other ferry routes, Northern Ireland will not be exempt from the charges, even though 90pc of commercial and passenger traffic goes by sea.

The Chamber said ferry firms will most likely have to pass the additional charges on.

Stena Line, which operates ferries to Belfast from Liverpool, Heysham in Lancashire and Cairnryan in Scotland, estimates that the scheme will increase its costs by £9m annually. That would average £15 for every HGV, van and car.

The Chamber said an initial 50pc deduction to match the carbon charges on voyages between Britain and Republic of Ireland will provide little relief.

That is because exporters in Northern Ireland compete mostly with firms in Britain rather than the province’s southern neighbour, to which they will be at a structural disadvantage.

UK firms have already spent millions on some of the most sustainably designed ferries, including methanol and rotor sail propulsion, leaving no financially viable way forward, it claimed.

The Chamber said: “The sector supports the UK’s climate goals, but cannot deliver meaningful emissions reduction without the necessary fuels, infrastructure, and clear guidance in place.

“Premature implementation risks higher costs for passengers and freight, with limited environmental gain. However, the Government is pressing ahead, leaving industry in an untenable position.”

The trade group urged ministers to delay introduction of the scheme while further assessments are carried out and said the revenue raised should be ring-fenced for investment in clean fuels and quayside power connections.

The Department for Energy Security and Net Zero said that it is for ship operators to decide how much of the expense of the ETS scheme to pass on, and that consumers should not see significant costs.

A spokesman said: “Pricing emissions is a cost-effective way to cut carbon emissions and encourage greener travel. We are working closely with industry on implementation.”

data.title: Train driver who fell asleep at controls drops attempt to overturn sacking
data.description:
A train driver who fell asleep at the controls has abandoned a trade union-backed attempt to overturn his sacking.

The unnamed Hull Trains driver was dismissed a year ago after experiencing “fatigue” at the controls of the company’s 125mph services on several occasions, The Telegraph previously disclosed.

These came to light when he mentioned them in passing during a work meeting, having previously been helped through similar health-related problems by Hull Trains’ managers.

Trade union Aslef called almost a full year of strikes to try to reverse the sacking but admitted defeat on Friday, with the former driver accepting he would not be reinstated.

The series of ineffectual Aslef strikes led to union membership among Hull Trains’ drivers dropping by more than a third during the past year, The Telegraph understands.

Fewer and fewer drivers were keeping up their Aslef membership, which averaged £373 per year in 2024, even as the union claimed that its members were repeatedly voting in favour of walkouts.

In a letter from last March explaining the driver’s dismissal, Martijn Gilbert, Hull Trains’ managing director, said: “The disclosures made by this driver ... presented us with a safety risk that we could not ignore.”

It continued: “We cannot be confident that they can be trusted to properly report safety matters ... so that we can support them and manage the risk.”

Under Mick Whelan, its previous general secretary, Aslef claimed Hull Trains was spreading “false and inaccurate” information.

Aslef and other unions claimed victory over the Conservative government in 2024 in a long-running row about anti-union laws designed to limit strikes’ ability to halt rail services.

Labour has repealed that law – the Minimum Service Levels Act 2023 – as part of pro-union reforms designed to boost membership numbers within such organisations, which in turn channel some of their members’ subscriptions into the political party’s funds.

Aslef failed to respond to a request for comment.

A spokesman for Hull Trains said: “We are pleased that our customers can continue to travel with confidence when booking with Hull Trains, following the positive outcome of our talks with Aslef.

“The safety of our customers and colleagues has and always will continue to be our number one priority.

“We look forward to welcoming record numbers of customers onboard in 2026 after achieving best long-distance train operator in the rail customer experience survey published in December.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Royal favourite Frog Bikes facing administration
data.description:
A children’s bike-maker favoured by Prince Louis is facing an uncertain future after filing an intention to appoint administrators.

Frog Bikes, founded in 2013 by husband-and-wife team Jerry and Shelley Lawson, made a court application through its law firm Shoosmiths on Friday to hire administrators, legal filings show.

Filing an intention to appoint an administrator is a legal step taken by companies to gain temporary protection from creditors. It can lead to a full-blown liquidation of a company or reorganisation of its finances.

Frog Bikes said the move was a “precautionary measure” to allow the company to keep trading while it explores “potential funding and restructuring solutions”.

Mr Lawson blamed the predicament on a “series of significant and well-documented challenges” – including higher employee taxes, Brexit, and low consumer confidence.

He added, “At the same time, tighter financial markets and reduced access to funding made it increasingly difficult for growing manufacturing businesses to secure the requisite working capital required to navigate these challenges.”

‘Continuing friction’ from Brexit

The Wales-based group has gained a cult following and won a dream endorsement when the Princess of Wales published a photo of her son riding one of the company’s products on his third birthday.

The business makes child-specific bikes after the founders said they struggled to find suitable ones for their own children.

Its lightweight aluminium bikes are made in Pontypool, Wales, where Mr and Mrs Lawson opened a 120,000-square-foot manufacturing facility.

After its success in the UK, it quickly became a market leader for affordable children’s bikes and rapidly expanded to more than 50 countries – including the US and Hong Kong.

However, Frog Bikes has recently warned that cumbersome export taxes were eating into profits.

In 2023, it lost £530,000 after blaming “continuing friction” from Brexit, which had made it harder to compete with European rivals.

However, in its most recent accounts for the year to February 2024, auditors said the group would continue as a going concern.

The report added that despite inflation falling at the time, consumers were still cautious in their spending on “big ticket purchases”, adding that there was competition from new entrants to the lightweight children’s bike market.

Bike market in turmoil

Cycling businesses have faced a difficult time as enthusiasm for the activity starts to wane.

A pandemic boom led many companies to expand rapidly, but after the interest faded, firms complained that the sudden drop in interest had left manufacturers with excess inventory.

Companies like Brompton Bicycles saw profits fall by more than 99pc in the year to March 2024. The following year, it reported pre-tax losses of £2m. At the time, the company described the bike market as being “in turmoil”.

Raleigh, another British brand, reported a loss of £30m in 2023, citing overstocked inventory following the pandemic.

Frog Bikes confirmed in a statement that the search for a new investor would continue.

Mr Lawson said: “All parties are committed to securing a positive outcome for the business, its employees and wider stakeholders.

“Frog Bikes’ priority remains supporting its employees, customers, suppliers, and retail partners while working with its advisers to determine the best possible outcome for all stakeholders.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: AI paranoia grips the City as chatbots take over
data.description:
Amadeo Alentorn still remembers the nervous questions he was asked when he began to let computer algorithms dictate stock market trades more than 20 years ago.

“Some investors would come to us and say, ‘What? You let computers decide which stocks to buy?’” says Alentorn, the head of systematic equities at Jupiter Asset Management.

“We said, ‘Yes, that’s exactly what we do. We build investment strategies that get coded up and then computers make those decisions.’”

Algorithmic trading has become the norm today for asset managers and other professional investors. But computers could soon be taking over even more of our financial lives.

Billions have been wiped off the market value of wealth management stocks this week as investors panic that artificial intelligence (AI) chatbots and programmes will soon be able to manage people’s money.

The sector is the latest to be hit by an AI panic – with software, law and accounting all suffering similar crashes in recent days.

More than £1bn has been wiped off the value of St James’s Place, the biggest wealth manager in Britain, this week, while rivals such as Quilter also tumbled. Stockbroking platforms have also crashed, with AJ Bell also falling.

The latest turmoil was triggered on Tuesday, when Altruist, a “unicorn” Los Angeles start-up, put out a press release announcing the launch of a new AI tax-strategy planner.

The programme, called Hazel, can write personalised tax strategies in minutes and can run interactive modelling on everything from the impact of a bonus to a home sale or retirement.

Jason Wenk, Altruist’s founder, says it “expands what a single advisor can handle, raises the bar on outcomes, and makes average advice a lot harder to justify”.

The tool has triggered mass panic in the stock market.

In the US, shares in wealth management and investment companies Charles Schwab, Raymond James and Morgan Stanley all tumbled. European equivalents Julius Baer, UBS and Amundi were also hit by the blanket selling.

“When you have something as fundamental as a rewriting of the economic landscape, driven by AI, the winners will only be known in years to come,” says Martin Frandsen, a portfolio manager at Principal Asset Management.

“Right now the market is being very non-discriminatory in terms of which companies will actually be the winners and losers.”

Feverish markets

The sell-off in wealth management is just the latest tremor of a broader AI earthquake hitting stock markets.

Software companies shed $830bn (£609bn) in six days this month after Anthropic announced a new plug-in for its Claude agent that can complete tasks across data analysis, marketing, legal and sales.

In turn came new fears for the private credit sector, which has invested heavily in software companies.

On Monday, insurance stocks tumbled after OpenAI approved the first AI insurance app on ChatGPT.

On Wednesday and Thursday, property companies companies similarly saw their share prices plummet on fears bots may replace estate agents.

Even the trucking and deliver industry has been hit. Shares were sent into tailspin after Algorhythm Holdings, a former maker of karaoke systems for cars, unveiled an AI tool designed to optimise freight efficiencies. The business triggered turmoil despite having a valuation of just $6m (£4.4m).

Christian Dery, of Capital Fund Management, warns: “Firms with generic business models, low barriers to entry and high risk of automation will not survive.”

The wealth sell-off is not so much because Hazel is a seismic technological breakthrough – it’s more because markets are feverish.

“You have all the fast money hedge funds selling whoever is seen as the next AI loser straight away,” says Emmanuel Cau, the head of European equity strategy at Barclays.

Analysts think the AI tool itself is not necessarily revolutionary.

Its updated software may be able to read payslips, bank statements, meeting notes, email and customer data but it is designed to help human advisors create personalised tax strategies, not replace them.

“It is very much fear driving this rather than fundamentals,” says Cau. “The market is moving to who is the next loser, the next victim, of this AI disruption phase.

“Things are moving very fast, and it’s very hard to keep pace and a lot of investors are getting hit. It is a massive pain trade.”

AI ‘reality is dawning’

While the tool itself may not be revolutionary, investors and the industry are alarmed at the pace at which the technology is evolving.

Susannah Streeter, chief investment strategist at the Wealth Club, says: “It’s not like the warning signs haven’t been here.

“It’s just that I don’t think the wider industry really believed that they could work quite so quickly, or couldn’t really understand exactly how they would be disruptive. I think that reality is dawning right now.”

For wealth managers and advisers, the fear is AI could ultimately drive down fees, which are typically opaque and vary significantly between firms. Analysts do not think that companies will go under, but their ability to charge may be significantly degraded and that has massive implications for their share prices.

“I think it has sent a bit of a shiver through the industry, this feeling that fees may be compressed in the future, this feeling that it’s going to be a lot harder to win customers and charge substantial fees for it,” says Streeter.

Wealth managers are trying to put a brave face on it.

Speaking at UBS’s Financial Services Conference on Tuesday, Jed Finn, the head of wealth management at Morgan Stanley, said: “It is the opposite. I think AI is going to enhance the quality of advice, and it’s going to help advisors scale and be able to serve more clients more effectively with the same set of resources.”

Human element trust

City folk argue that there are limits on how far people will be willing to use ChatGPT or other AI tools to make investment decisions.

“Everyone has heard about the so-called hallucinations of large language models, that ChatGPT-type applications can sometimes give you a very incorrect answer with a lot of confidence,” says Alentorn.

“When it comes to important, potentially life-changing decisions like the deployment of capital, a catastrophic error would really have a big impact on someone’s life.”

He adds: “Clearly what’s important there is the role of the human being in creating and designing those tools and making sure that the right safeguards are in place.”

There is more to the human element than just making sure AI is fit for purpose, too.

“For all of the technological and efficiency improvements that AI has the potential to bring to the wealth industry, we don’t think it, or other automated solutions, can ever fully replace the ‘human empathy’ and ‘trust’ side of being a financial adviser,” says David McCann, an analyst at Deutsche Bank.

However, he acknowledges that “hand holding” during market downturns may be less important to “more digitally native, younger individuals” – especially as they become more confident and trusting of AI.

It is a possibility that a 25-year-old who is starting to build a portfolio today may never feel they need a human wealth manager.

“Think about all of those people who’ve already got a relationship with their chatbot. They’re asking them for relationship advice, for health advice,” says Streeter.

“It’s not a great leap to expect them to be trusting a chatbot to give them the best financial advice.”

data.title: Britain’s hapless water watchdog is asleep on the job again
data.description:
Just when you thought things couldn’t get any worse for the parched residents of Tunbridge Wells and surrounding towns, here comes Ofwat to the rescue.

With some households in the region facing their second bout of water shortages in the space of a few weeks at the hands of South East Water, some must have been praying for a miracle. Yet the comically late arrival of Britain’s regulator is the equivalent of sending in Dad’s Army.

Responsibility for the fiasco that has unfolded ultimately resides with South East Water’s equally inept, overpaid chief executive Dave Hinton and the board of nodding dog directors who have somehow seen fit to allow him to remain in post.

But for an enforced and humiliating appearance in front of a parliamentary committee of senior MPs, Hinton has been completely missing in action throughout this debacle.

Nor is this catalogue of failures a one-off. Under its current boss, South East Water has a thoroughly woeful track record of failing in its basic duty to keep the taps running for 2.3 million customers in Kent, Sussex, Surrey, Hampshire and Berkshire.

This is the second officially-declared major incident in South East Water territory in as many months, with customers in Tunbridge Wells, Kent still facing disruption after weeks. Thousands of households were also hit by shortages in 2022 and 2023.

As my colleague Patrick Galbraith and I point out in a separate piece for The Telegraph, South East Water is well on its way to claiming the crown of Britain’s worst utility from industry basket case Thames Water, which really does take some doing given its current sorry state.

Mr Hinton should have been shown the door long ago, but he’s being kept in post by the company’s unforgivably meek independent directors, led by equally absentee chairman Chris Train, who knows a thing or two about companies that fall short.

Train is also the chairman of Sellafield, which was fined for serious cybersecurity failings in 2024. The following year, the Commons Public Accounts Committee (Pac) criticised the nuclear power plant for “failure, cost-overruns and continuing safety concerns.”

Yet, the idea that Ofwat’s intervention will make much of a difference really is the stuff of fantasy.

Britain is awash with dire regulators who are meant to protect consumers from cowboy firms. The Financial Conduct Authority was branded “incompetent at best, dishonest at worst”, by MPs and peers in 2024.

Meanwhile, Pac found in 2022 that failures at Ofgem had come “at a considerable cost to billpayers” after the collapse of a staggering 29 energy suppliers in less than 18 months.

The only regulator that has shown some bite is the Competition and Markets Authority. And its reward for standing up for consumers? A thorough defanging at the hands of ministers who claimed it had become an impediment to economic growth.

Otherwise, in a crowded field, Ofwat must be a contender for the most ineffective industry policeman of all time. It is a watchdog that has repeatedly demonstrated little, if any, willingness to properly hold the industry to account, as South East knows only too well from considerable experience.

The vast bulk of Britain’s waterways have become a public health emergency, and water shortages are now commonplace – the result of extreme underinvestment in the country’s decrepit pipes and sewers by an industry that has routinely prioritised generous executive pay and investor dividends.

On every conceivable measure and metric, companies have been failing consumers on a grand scale for years.

Ultimately, the buck stops with greedy, irresponsible suppliers and shareholders. Yet, years of regulatory apathy have allowed something as basic and fundamental as the provision of drinking water to be turned into a giant and lucrative experiment in financial engineering at the expense of customers.

On the occasions when Ofwat does act, it’s like watching a snail wake up from a coma.

South East Water is already the subject of a regulatory investigation – one dating back to 2023 that still hasn’t been resolved – so what chance of a quick verdict?

As Mike Martin, the MP for Tunbridge Wells, points out: “It’s obvious that South East Water has breached the terms of the license.”

In the face of Ofwat’s impotence, is it any wonder the company has been getting away with it for ages?

Four major outages have been racked up on Hinton’s watch, and South East Water has ranked in the bottom three companies for disruptions every year since he took over.

2023 was particularly shocking, with the company recording the most interruptions and outages of any supplier in England and Wales.

Meanwhile, South East has failed to meet more than 40pc of its targets every year for the last three years. Last year, just 13 of its 38 annual targets were hit. Unsurprisingly, Ofwat penalties of a few million pounds here and there have had little effect.

Financially, it is a mess, too. Total losses since 2022 stand at £130m, the result of profits being consistently wiped out by the cost of servicing £1.3bn of borrowings. 

At times, debt interest and dividends have eclipsed the money that has been spent mending leaky pipes and other vital infrastructure.

Hinton, meanwhile, has earned nearly £2.3m since taking charge in 2020 and is in line for a 30pc pay rise, plus a £400,000 long-term “service bonus” regardless of performance. Talk about reward for failure.

It is an untenable situation that has long been crying out for a supervisor with real mettle.

Lynn Parker, senior director for enforcement at Ofwat, is right to say that “the last six weeks have been miserable for businesses and households across Kent and Sussex” – but why did it take so long for Ofwat to do anything?

Even now, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that Ofwat only acted because Emma Reynolds, the Environment Secretary, had called publicly for it to step in.

While the regulator has dithered, senior MPs, together with Martin, have done a brilliant job of holding Hinton and colleagues to account.

Ofwat, on the other hand, is hard to take seriously. The threat of penalties, including the annulment of South East’s licence, is somewhat hollow to say the least, given the Government’s plans to abolish Ofwat, the Drinking Water Inspectorate and the Environment Agency, and replace it with a single water regulator responsible for the entire water system.

It is a welcomed move. Long-suffering customers deserve better and the disbanding of the current abject regime is a rare opportunity to create a watchdog with genuine clout. Labour cannot afford to get it wrong.

data.title: Meet Labour’s new cost of living champion… a former Tory from Wales’s wealthiest family
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer is facing backlash from Labour MPs following the appointment of a millionaire supermarket tycoon as his cost of living tsar.

One backbencher likened the appointment to hiring a butcher to provide food to vegans.

Last week, No 10 appointed Lord Walker of Broxton, executive chairman of supermarket chain Iceland, to the position.

Lord Walker, the son of Iceland founder Sir Malcolm Walker and part of the highest-taxpaying family in Wales, will report directly to the Prime Minister and “bring real-world business experience” to the Government’s attempt to tackle the cost of living crisis.

Labour MPs have criticised the appointment of the former Tory donor, which follows a series of scandals over Sir Keir’s choices of senior political appointees. One Labour MP told The Telegraph it “whiffed” of the Prime Minister’s poor political judgment.

Lord Mandelson, who was made British ambassador to the US under Sir Keir, resigned from the Labour Party and left the House of Lords last week after further revelations about his friendship with convicted paedophile Jeffrey Epstein emerged.

On Sunday, Morgan McSweeney, the Prime Minister’s chief of staff, quit over his role in pushing for Lord Mandelson’s appointment. Sir Keir’s fourth director of communications, Tim Allan, resigned the next day.

On Tuesday, Lord Doyle, Sir Keir’s former spin doctor who served in Downing Street until last year, was suspended from the party over his links to convicted paedophile Sean Morton, a former Labour councillor.

Another Labour MP said: “This just feels like a long line of worse and worse decisions – it’s the last of desperate days, I think. A new ‘cost of living champion’, it’s groan-inducing but it doesn’t surprise me.

“The conflict of interest concerns me. Lord Walker’s recommendations going through No 10, into policy – how do we know whether his recommendations are particularly good for his business? It’s hotline politics.”

Lord Walker, who has expressed ambitions to be prime minister one day, has been accused of “self-serving” politics after switching allegiances from the Conservative Party to Labour. He donated nearly £10,000 to the Tories in 2020, leaving the party in 2023 just months after he was approved as a potential parliamentary candidate.

His bid for election as an MP was shot down by senior Conservative figures under Rishi Sunak, it is understood. Tory insiders said Lord Walker’s “evident self-serving” change of heart came after a losing bid for power within the party.

The tycoon’s passion to achieve net zero by 2040 “did not find favour with those at the top of the Conservative Party”, he explained in The Guardian at the time.

Lord Walker was made a life peer by Sir Keir in December – one of 25 new Labour appointees who sit in the House of Lords.

Dame Andrea Jenkyns, of Reform UK, said: “The appointment of Richard Walker as a ‘consumer champion’ is a masterclass in the very political cronyism the public is sick of.

“This is a man who was banging on the door of the Conservative Party for a seat just a few years ago and when he didn’t get what he wanted, he suddenly discovered a ‘passion’ for the Labour front bench.”

Lord Walker has also faced scrutiny for his business’s use of taxpayers funds. The supermarket heir was managing director of Iceland when it refused to return £40m in Covid support loans to the taxpayer.

The tax breaks were designed to help struggling companies weather closures during the pandemic – but supermarkets, which stayed open and received a sales boost during the period, also received the relief. Tesco, Sainsbury’s, Morrisons, Asda and Aldi agreed to hand back more than £2bn of the tax relief in December 2020. Iceland did not.

Sales at the chain surged more than a fifth in the six months to September 2020. Iceland said revenue was up 22pc to £1.7bn in the 24 weeks to September 11, compared to a 6.6pc rise at Tesco.

“We simply can’t afford to return it,” Lord Walker wrote at the time. He said it was used to pay for personal protective equipment, hand sanitiser and cover for self-isolating staff.

Iceland later brought in the largest price increases of all major supermarkets. The frozen food chain recorded a 9.4pc rise in prices between November 2021 and November 2022, according to research by comparison website Trolley at the time. This compared to a 1.9pc increase by Co-op, 4.9pc by Waitrose and 6.3pc for Aldi shoppers.

A veteran Labour MP said: “Appointing a rich guy who runs a food retailer when food prices have gone through the roof is like appointing a butcher to provide food to vegans.”

After the 2024 Budget, Lord Walker told businesses to “stop wallowing” – but later criticised Rachel Reeves’s tax rises and accused Labour ministers in December last year of thinking “profit is a dirty word”.

Daisy Cooper, the Liberal Democrat deputy leader, said these comments made him an unsuitable choice for the role.

She said: “Small businesses have already been hammered with the jobs tax, higher business rates bills and extra red tape requirements, so appointing a cost of living tsar whose only advice is for businesses to ‘stop wallowing’ is yet another kick in the teeth.”

Others felt the Iceland boss would bring relevant experience to the role. One Labour backbencher said the appointment was “fine – anyone who can address how to get prices down is welcome”.

Lord Walker has long been outspoken about the cost of living crisis. Announcing his new role, the Government praised Iceland’s interest-free microcredit scheme which it said had mitigated food poverty for tens of thousands of customers.

The tycoon said he would focus on “everyday areas that eat into household budgets and where consumers get a rough deal” in his new position.

Lord Walker joined the Iceland team as a shop-floor worker in 2012, which he called “the best year of his life” and “humbling” – though he admitted to The Sunday Times he was paid more than other shop workers.

He published “The Green Grocer” in April 2021, his sustainability bible for other businesses to follow Iceland’s lead. A year later, he confessed to being a “hypocrite” over the company’s withdrawal on a pledge to remove plastic from its shelves in 2023.

Meanwhile he came under fire for his use of the company’s helicopter, spotted around the country on 48 different days within the year his book was published, according to The Times in 2021. Lord Walker made “absolutely no apology” for using the helicopter, he said at the time.

The Walker family are the highest taxpayers in Wales and 28th highest in the UK at a sum of £56.4m, according to The Sunday Times 2026 taxpayers’ list.

Lord Walker owns three properties and a share of four more, including three in Spain.

His father, Sir Malcolm, who owns a 82ft yacht, has long defended the Walker family wealth, telling The Telegraph in 2014: “My personal wealth is nothing compared to the wealth we’ve created.”

Asked at the time about his estimated £215m fortune, Sir Malcolm said: “It’s family wealth. My kids have got most of my money. It’s what you do, isn’t it? Diversify your wealth.”

Lord Walker once claimed that as the boss’s son he worked “doubly hard”.

Lord Walker and Iceland declined to comment. Sir Malcolm Walker was contacted for comment.

data.title: How mobile phone providers are getting away with 22pc price rises
data.description:
Have you been hit by a mid-contract price increase on your phone bill? Get in touch with us at money@telegraph.co.uk*.

Mobile phone network providers have been accused of “milking” customers with mid-contract price rises as high as 22pc.

Last year regulator Ofcom banned phone providers from including inflation-linked or percentage-based price rises in new contracts over concerns that the practice left customers with uncertainty about the level of future increases.

Customer contracts must now state at the point of sale what any future price rises will be in pounds and pence.

But consumer experts have warned that the new rules have resulted in even bigger increases for customers compared to when bills typically rose by inflation plus 3.9pc.

One O2 customer told The Telegraph that his phone bill rose mid-contract by 22pc last April, after the provider increased prices by £1.80 across the board. This far outstripped the 7.3pc increase he would have seen under the old system.

Consumer group Which? has warned that customers on cheaper tariffs could be disproportionately impacted by fixed uplifts, as they would likely see larger percentage increases to their bill. For example, a £2.50 rise for a £10 tariff amounts to a 25pc uplift, whereas the same £2.50 increase on a £30 tariff equates to a rise of just 8pc.

Martin Lewis, the Money Saving Expert, previously said that Ofcom’s new rules were “flaccid” and that they “effectively embed” above-inflation mid-contract price rises across the industry.

He added: “I don’t understand why we don’t grasp what is by far the simplest and most effective solution: ban firms from increasing prices above inflation during a fixed-term contract.”

Britain’s biggest mobile phone providers – Vodafone and Three (which recently merged), Sky, Virgin Media, O2 and EE (which is a part of BT) – signed a new consumer charter on Wednesday that the Government has said will prevent surprise phone bill increases. However, the policy change still won’t ban mid-term above-inflation increases.

The agreement was in part drawn up in response to an announcement by O2 last year that it would increase bills by £2.50 from April 2026, more than the £1.80 customers had signed up to.

Ofcom said at the time that the uplift went against the spirit of its new rules, which the provider was able to circumvent by allowing customers to leave mid-contract without a penalty within 30 days.

Firms such as Sky include “variable pricing” structures in tariffs which are allowed because the customer has a right to leave within 30 days of being notified.

New consumer rights have ‘backfired’

Business consultant Andy Reed was among those stung by a mid-contract price rise last April.

The 64-year-old, an O2 customer, initially signed up to a one-year SIM-only tariff at £7.99 a month in July 2024. Nine months later, his bill was increased to £9.79, a 22pc rise.

He said: “The fact that my bill has gone up by 22pc in less than a year is frankly outrageous.

“Ofcom’s new rules are simpler but it means operators can exploit it to make more money. People know what their new bill is going to be, but when you look at it in percentage terms, it’s the operators milking their lowest-paying customers. It’s backfired.”

An O2 spokesman said: “An annual rise of £2.50 a month – around 8p a day – continues to represent excellent value for services that customers are using more than ever before.

“We’ve again frozen prices on handset repayment plans and are investing £700m into our mobile network this year to ensure we meet growing demand and give our customers the fast and reliable connectivity they rely on.”

‘Customers ripped off’

All four of Britain’s largest mobile phone network providers are expected to raise prices for most customers under contract by a fixed amount this spring.

The exact rise can vary depending on when you signed your contract, which provider you use and how much data you pay for. Vodafone, Three and EE increase prices in line with what customers agreed to when they signed their contract.

Dr Luke Evans, a Conservative MP, said: “It’s more than discouraging to hear, after Ofcom introduced rules to address this, that some increases are at a higher percentage than when increases were set to inflation plus 3.9pc. It’s no wonder that customers, who are tied into a contract, feel they are being completely ripped off.”

The Telegraph has seen the bill of one customer who is under contract with Three which will rise from £7 to £8.25 in April, representing a 17pc increase. One EE customer’s bill is poised to increase by 9pc to £28.80 in March.

Ernest Doku, of comparison website Uswitch, said: “The best defence is to keep track of your contract end date as switching the moment you’re free allows you to reset your bill and avoid these increases compounding on top of expensive out-of-contract rates.”

Citizens Advice has now called for an outright ban on mid-contract price rises.

The charity’s Anne Pardoe said: “When inflation was at an all-time high, Ofcom was right to act to protect consumers from unpredictable, inflation-linked telecoms price hikes mid-contract. But what started as a well-intentioned reform has left many people facing bigger price rises.

“By focusing on pricing transparency from telecom providers, the regulator has inadvertently made the affordability problem worse. Greater clarity shouldn’t come at greater cost.”

An Ofcom spokesman said: “These commitments build on the significant protections already in place in our competitive telecoms markets – from simpler switching and social tariffs to warning customers when contracts are ending and protecting people from volatile and unpredictable inflation.

“At the same time, broadband and mobile companies are investing heavily in upgrading and expanding their networks, delivering greater choice and better quality while average prices have been falling in real terms.

A VodafoneThree spokesman said: “To give customers greater certainty and transparency, in line with Ofcom’s guidelines, we moved to a pounds-and-pence approach where any mid-contract price rise is clearly set out upfront at the point of sale, so customers know exactly what they will pay across their contract term, and that fixed amount does not change during the contract.”

An EE spokesman said: “We remain committed to Ofcom’s requirement to display upfront pounds-and-pence charges across the full length of a customer’s contract. We will be contacting every customer to explain what price change means for them and the options available. We are not adjusting the annual price change for customers within the minimum term of their contract.”

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph processing your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.
